payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Assamese (code=as, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
MATERNAL SAFETY: clinical content must be age-plausible for persona age=22. Routine ANC/delivery typically ages 18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC/PNC/contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy as a routine story.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Assam, district=Tinsukia.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Bengali for Assamese.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 22
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Assam
- district: Tinsukia
- first_language: Assamese
- clinical_persona_summary: A 22-year-old female currently married, retired homemaker with primary education, residing in an urban area of Tinsukia, Assam. Her first language is Assamese, with functional Hindi proficiency. Clinically, she may exhibit lifestyle cues related to her cultural background, such as a diet rich in traditional Assamese cuisine and potential engagement in community activities like Bihu celebrations. No specific information on sports, travel, or detailed culinary habits is provided.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

পদ্ধতি / ফলাফল:
ৰোগীজনীৰ বাবে নিৰ্বাচিত নিম্ন ট্ৰেন্সভাৰ্ছ চিজেৰিয়ান চেক্সনৰ পৰামৰ্শ লোৱা হৈছিল গৰ্ভধাৰণৰ 39 সপ্তাহত ব্ৰীচ প্ৰেজেণ্টেচনৰ বাবে। অস্ত্ৰোপচাৰৰ পূৰ্বৰ মূল্যায়নত স্বাভাৱিক ভাইটেলছ, পৰ্যাপ্ত হিম’গ্লবিনৰ মাত্ৰা আৰু কোনো বিৰোধিতা পোৱা নগ’ল। জটিলতা নোহোৱাকৈ এনেস্থেচিয়া প্ৰদান কৰা হৈছিল। নূন্যতম তেজৰ লোকচানৰ সৈতে নিম্ন অংশত জৰায়ুৰ কটা কৰা হৈছিল। 3.2 কেজি ওজনৰ এজনীয়া ল’ৰা সন্তান জন্ম হৈছিল ক্ৰমে 1 আৰু 5 মিনিটত 8 আৰু 9 এপগাৰ স্ক’ৰৰ সৈতে। অক্ষত আৱৰণৰ সৈতে জৰায়ুৰ নলী (Placenta) স্বতঃস্ফূৰ্তভাৱে ওলাই আহিল। শোষণযোগ্য চিলাইৰ সৈতে জৰায়ু বন্ধ কৰা কাৰ্য দুটা স্তৰত কৰা হৈছিল। আনুমানিক তেজৰ লোকচান 250 ml। স্থিৰ অৱস্থাত ৰোগীজনক পুনৰুদ্ধাৰ কক্ষলৈ স্থানান্তৰ কৰা হৈছিল।

payload.generated_text_en:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Tinsukia District Hospital]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Borah]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priyanka Sharma]]
Procedure / findings: Patient presented for elective low transverse cesarean section at 39 weeks gestation for breech presentation. Pre-operative assessment revealed normal vitals, adequate hemoglobin levels, and no contraindications. Anesthesia administered without complications. Uterine incision made in lower segment with minimal blood loss. Male infant delivered weighing 3.2 kg with Apgar scores 8 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes respectively. Placenta delivered spontaneously with intact membranes. Uterine closure performed in two layers with absorbable sutures. Estimated blood loss 250 ml. Patient transferred to recovery room in stable condition.

payload.persona: Afreen Badeghar is a curious yet practical matriarch who meticulously budgets while they embrace a laid‑back, sometimes disorganized lifestyle, finding solace in garden work, qawwalis, and quiet prayer.
payload.cultural_background: Afreen belongs to the rural Muslim community of Chitradurga in Karnataka, where the local culture blends Kannada customs with Islamic traditions. payload.linguistic_background: Afreen's primary language is English, which she acquired through community interactions, radio, and occasional school exposure, but she cannot read or write in any script. BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Karnataka, district=Chitradurga.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Latin for English.

PLACEMENT: Include [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] as the reporting radiologist (e.g. Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]]) — do not leave the doctor name as untagged prose.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 59
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Karnataka
- district: Chitradurga
- first_language: English
- clinical_persona_summary: A 59-year-old female, currently married, retired homemaker with no formal education, residing in a rural area of Chitradurga, Karnataka. Her primary language is English, with conversational skills in Kannada and Marathi, and she follows a traditional lifestyle with a focus on budgeting and household management. Clinically, she has limited health literacy and may require simplified communication for medical instructions.

Document format guide for Radiology / Imaging Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Findings: <radiology prose, untagged>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Radiology report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Chitradurga District Hospital]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|Savitri Bai]] [[AGE|59]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-CHIT-2024-001]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-CHIT-2024-0815-001]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Kavita Reddy]]
Findings: Plain radiograph of the right lower limb demonstrates a well-defined, lytic lesion in the distal femur, measuring approximately 4.5 x 3.8 cm, with a thin sclerotic rim. The remainder of the bony structures of the right lower limb is unremarkable.

payload.linguistic_background: Afreen's primary language is English, which she acquired through community interactions, radio, and occasional school exposure, but she cannot read or write in any script. She also has conversational command of Kannada and Marathi, sufficient for grocery shopping, market bargaining, and participating in local religious functions. Her illiteracy means she relies heavily on oral instructions and trusted neighbours for any written tasks.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Afreen excels in managing a large household, planning meals for the family, and cooking traditional South Indian Muslim cuisine such as biryani, kosambari, and various types of sweets. She is adept at preparing homemade pickles and chutneys using age‑old family recipes, and she often creates herbal remedies for common ailments based on local knowledge. Additionally, she efficiently budgets household expenses, coordinates community religious events, and mentors younger women in domestic skills.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ER Triage & Nursing Notes for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into English (code=en, script=Latin). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Karnataka, district=Chitradurga.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Latin for English.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once in the arrival/RTA/ambulance line. Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 59
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Karnataka
- district: Chitradurga
- first_language: English
- clinical_persona_summary: A 59-year-old female, currently married, retired homemaker with no formal education, residing in a rural area of Chitradurga, Karnataka. Her primary language is English, with conversational fluency in Kannada and Marathi, and she follows a traditional lifestyle with limited literacy. Clinically, she exhibits a conservative, budget-conscious approach to health, engaging in low-intensity activities like morning yoga and listening to religious music.

Document format guide for ER Triage & Nursing Notes (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|...]]
Arrived by ambulance / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|...]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Vitals / acuity: <short notes>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Chitradurga District Hospital]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0815-001]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Savitri Bai]] [[AGE|59]] [[GENDER|Female]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|05]]
Arrived by ambulance [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|KA01AB1234]]
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ramesh Kumar]]
Vitals: BP 140/90, Pulse 98, Temp 101.2°F, RR 20, SpO2 96% on room air. Patient appears anxious, pale, diaphoretic.

Chief complaint: Severe abdominal pain for 2 days with vomiting and weakness.

History of present illness: Patient reports sudden onset of sharp lower abdominal pain 48 hours ago, initially mild but progressively worsening. Associated with two episodes of vomiting, non-bilious. Reports decreased appetite and generalized weakness. No fever initially, but developed low-grade fever yesterday. No similar episodes in past.

Past medical history: Hypertension for 8 years, type 2 diabetes mellitus for 5 years. Taking medications irregularly. No known surgeries.

Physical examination: Abdomen tender in lower quadrants, guarding present, bowel sounds decreased. No rebound tenderness. Mild hepatomegaly noted.

Investigations ordered: CBC, LFT, RBS, abdominal X-ray, ECG.

Plan: Admit for observation, IV fluids, analgesics, antihypertensive and diabetic medications. Monitor vitals q4h. Surgical consult if condition worsens.

payload.professional_persona: Afreen Badeghar, a 59‑year‑old household manager and community event coordinator, leverages her knack for budgeting, traditional cooking, and pickling while they flexibly juggle daily chores despite a tendency to be loosely organized.
payload.linguistic_persona: Afreen Badeghar communicates in functional English for everyday needs, switches to conversational Kannada and Marathi at the market, and they rely on oral instructions and trusted neighbours for any written tasks due to illiteracy, reflecting a practical curiosity tempered by occasional disorganization.
payload.sports_persona: Afreen Badeghar enjoys gentle yoga stretches each morning and follows the Royal Challengers Bangalore cricket team, and they occasionally walk to the nearby market for fresh produce, but they rarely join organized sports, preferring low‑impact activities that suit their relaxed schedule.
payload.arts_persona: Afreen Badeghar relishes listening to qawwalis of the Sabri Brothers and bhajans of Anup Jalota on the radio, watches Kannada serials like 'Mareez' and Bollywood classics starring Amitabh Bachchan, and they find the melodic rhythm of these performances soothing during their quiet evenings.
payload.travel_persona: Afreen Badeghar prefers modest trips, having explored Mysore Palace and Hampi’s ruins with their children, and they dream of a pilgrimage‑style journey to Hyderabad for a major Islamic conference, though family budget constraints keep the plan tentative.
payload.culinary_persona: Afreen Badeghar excels at cooking fragrant biryani, tangy kosambari, and a variety of pickles using mustard seeds, fenugreek, raw mango, and tamarind, while they favor sweet treats such as sheer khurma, rose‑flavoured barfi, and saffron‑infused jalebi.
payload.persona: Afreen Badeghar is a curious yet practical matriarch who meticulously budgets while they embrace a laid‑back, sometimes disorganized lifestyle, finding solace in garden work, qawwalis, and quiet prayer.
payload.cultural_background: Afreen belongs to the rural Muslim community of Chitradurga in Karnataka, where the local culture blends Kannada customs with Islamic traditions. Growing up in a joint family, she experienced a lifestyle shaped by agricultural cycles, community festivals like Eid, and a strong emphasis on oral storytelling. Her family's heritage reflects a mix of South Indian rural values and the modest, decorous norms of the Muslim faith.
payload.linguistic_background: Afreen's primary language is English, which she acquired through community interactions, radio, and occasional school exposure, but she cannot read or write in any script. She also has conversational command of Kannada and Marathi, sufficient for grocery shopping, market bargaining, and participating in local religious functions. Her illiteracy means she relies heavily on oral instructions and trusted neighbours for any written tasks.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Afreen excels in managing a large household, planning meals for the family, and cooking traditional South Indian Muslim cuisine such as biryani, kosambari, and various types of sweets. She is adept at preparing homemade pickles and chutneys using age‑old family recipes, and she often creates herbal remedies for common ailments based on local knowledge. Additionally, she efficiently budgets household expenses, coordinates community religious events, and mentors younger women in domestic skills.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Household management', 'Traditional Muslim cooking', 'Pickle and preserve preparation', 'Herbal remedy formulation', 'Household budgeting', 'Community religious event coordination', 'Mentoring younger women in domestic skills']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Afreen enjoys listening to qawwalis and devotional bhajans on the radio, tending to her small vegetable and flower garden, and knitting soft scarves for her grandchildren. She likes watching Kannada television serials and occasional Bollywood movies, participates in the local mahila sangh meetings where she shares recipes, and solves word puzzles broadcast on the local FM station. On quiet evenings she also practices simple yoga stretches for flexibility.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to qawwalis', 'Gardening', 'Knitting scarves', 'Watching Kannada TV serials', 'Mahila sangh meetings', 'Solving radio word puzzles', 'Practicing yoga']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Afreen aspires to enroll in a government‑run adult literacy program to acquire basic reading and writing skills, which she believes will enhance her independence. She also dreams of converting her expertise in pickling and sweets into a modest home‑based cottage industry, aiming to supply nearby village markets and earn a modest supplement for her family. Additionally, she wishes to volunteer as a Qur’an recitation mentor for children at the local madrasa and hopes to travel to Hyderabad once to attend a major Islamic conference.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 59-year-old female, currently married, retired homemaker with no formal education, residing in a rural area of Chitradurga, Karnataka. Her primary language is English, with conversational skills in Kannada and Marathi. She follows a traditional lifestyle, engaging in garden work and listening to qawwalis.

You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.

payload.generated_text:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Lakshmi Bai]] [[AGE|59]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0815-001]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|lakshmi.bai@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Chitradurga District Hospital]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Good morning doctor. I have been having a persistent cough for the past three weeks, and it's getting worse. Sometimes I cough up yellow phlegm, and I've been feeling feverish in the evenings. I've also lost about 5 kilograms without trying. I'm worried this might be serious.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ramesh Kumar]]: Good morning Lakshmi Bai. Thank you for sharing these symptoms. The cough with yellow phlegm, evening fever, and weight loss are concerning signs that we need to investigate properly. Have you been in contact with anyone who has had tuberculosis, or do you work in conditions where you might be exposed to dust or smoke?

Patient: I live in a village near Chitradurga, and my husband works in a stone quarry. He comes home tired and dusty every day. I also have diabetes for the last 8 years, which is controlled with metformin. My blood sugar readings have been around 120-140 fasting.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ramesh Kumar]]: Thank you for that information. Your diabetes history is important because it can increase your risk for tuberculosis. Given your symptoms and diabetes, I strongly recommend we do a sputum test to check for TB bacteria. I also want to check your blood sugar levels more frequently since infections can affect glucose control. Have you noticed any night sweats or shortness of breath?

Patient: Yes, I do sweat a lot at night, and sometimes I feel breathless when climbing the small stairs to my kitchen. I'm also feeling very tired all the time, even after sleeping well.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ramesh Kumar]]: These are classic TB symptoms, especially with your diabetes. I'm going to arrange for you to come to the hospital tomorrow morning for a sputum test and chest X-ray. We'll also check your blood sugar levels. If the tests confirm TB, we'll start you on the standard treatment regimen. In the meantime, please continue your diabetes medications and try to rest as much as possible. Is there anything else you'd like to ask me?

Patient: Will the treatment be very expensive? I'm worried about the costs since my husband's work has been irregular lately.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ramesh Kumar]]: Don't worry about the cost. The government provides free TB treatment including all medications and tests. You'll also receive nutritional support. We have a social worker who can help you with any financial assistance you might need. The most important thing is to start treatment early. We'll take care of everything at the hospital.

payload.professional_persona: Shobha Toravi is a 45‑year‑old tailor who blends traditional motifs with contemporary cuts, using their vivid imagination to design bespoke wedding and festival wear while juggling day‑to‑day shop management, yet they often wrestle with deadlines because they prefer spontaneous creativity over strict schedules.
payload.linguistic_persona: Shobha Toravi grew up mastering English through daily market chatter and Hindi for wider client communication, now they converse fluently in both, read simple labels and market signs, but their reading and writing remain modest due to limited formal schooling.
payload.sports_persona: Shobha Toravi enjoys early morning walks along the Mangalore shoreline, plays casual badminton at the local community centre, and avidly follows Royal Challengers Bangalore cricket matches, using sport as a brief escape from the tailoring studio.
payload.arts_persona: Shobha Toravi draws inspiration from classical Hindustani music, especially listening to Pandit Jasraj and Zakir Hussain while sketching, admires the vibrant paintings of M.F. Husain, frequently attends Yakshagana performances featuring legendary dancer P.K. Nagappa, and enjoys classic Indian cinema starring Amitabh Bachchan and Savithri, letting these artistic experiences enrich their design aesthetic.
payload.travel_persona: Shobha Toravi prefers short, well‑planned getaways to nearby hill stations like Coorg and coastal retreats such as Goa, but occasionally indulges in spontaneous weekend trips to the Kerala backwaters, balancing limited savings with a yearning for a future Europe adventure.
payload.culinary_persona: Shobha Toravi relishes spicy Mangalorean fare—neer dosa with fish curry, coconut rice, and sweet Mysore pak—preferring to savor them at local eateries, and occasionally experiments with fresh basil and curry leaves in simple home‑cooked dishes, though they rarely cook elaborate meals.
payload.persona: Shobha Toravi is a creatively restless tailor who dreams big, worries often, and juggles artistry with the daily grind of a modest shop.
payload.cultural_background: Shobha Toravi is a Hindu woman from the urban centre of Dakshina Kannada, Karnataka, a coastal region known for its blend of Kannada, Tulu and Konkani cultures. Although her family primarily follows the customs of mainland Karnataka—celebrating Ugadi, Deepavali, and local temple festivals—her day‑to‑day life is also shaped by the cosmopolitan atmosphere of Mangalore, where Hindi and English are common lingua‑francas for trade. Growing up in a modest household with limited formal schooling, she absorbed cultural knowledge through community gatherings, family rituals, and the visual richness of regional textiles and artisanal crafts. Her imaginative nature draws her to the colourful masquerade of local festivals and the abstract motifs that appear in coastal folk art.
payload.linguistic_background: Shobha's first language is English; she learned it informally through daily interactions with customers and neighbours. Despite having only below‑primary formal schooling, she can converse fluently, understand basic written English, and uses it to read garment labels, pattern guides and mobile messages. Her second language is Hindi, which she speaks comfortably to a wider Indian clientele and reads simple Hindi scripts such as market signs and devotional pamphlets. She has limited reading and writing proficiency in both languages due to her schooling level, but compensates with strong visual and practical skills.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Shobha is skilled in tailoring and dressmaking, with a keen eye for intricate embroidery and fabric selection. She can draft simple patterns, take precise measurements, and execute custom fittings. Her work includes client consultation, colour coordination, and adapting traditional motifs into contemporary designs. She also manages the day‑to‑day operations of her modest tailoring business, handling budgeting, supplies procurement, and improvisational problem‑solving (jugaad) when faced with material constraints.

payload.hobbies_and_interests: Shobha enjoys sketching abstract designs, experimenting with new fabric patterns, and listening to classical Hindustani music while she works. She often attends local craft fairs and folk dance performances for inspiration. In her leisure time, she likes taking walks along the Mangalore beach, gardening with native herbs, reading devotional stories, and watching classic Indian cinema. payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Shobha aims to grow her modest tailoring unit into a recognized boutique that specializes in bespoke wedding and festival wear, blending contemporary aesthetics with traditional motifs. payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

payload.linguistic_persona: Shobha Toravi grew up mastering English through daily market chatter and Hindi for wider client communication, now they converse fluently in both, read simple labels and market signs, but their reading and writing remain modest due to limited formal schooling.
payload.sports_persona: Shobha Toravi enjoys early morning walks along the Mangalore shoreline, plays casual badminton at the local community centre, and avidly follows Royal Challengers Bangalore cricket matches, using sport as a brief escape from the tailoring studio.
payload.arts_persona: Shobha Toravi draws inspiration from classical Hindustani music, especially listening to Pandit Jasraj and Zakir Hussain while sketching, admires the vibrant paintings of M.F. Husain, frequently attends Yakshagana performances featuring legendary dancer P.K. Nagappa, and enjoys classic Indian cinema starring Amitabh Bachchan and Savithri, letting these artistic experiences enrich their design aesthetic.
payload.travel_persona: Shobha Toravi prefers short, well‑planned getaways to nearby hill stations like Coorg and coastal retreats such as Goa, but occasionally indulges in spontaneous weekend trips to the Kerala backwaters, balancing limited savings with a yearning for a future Europe adventure.
payload.culinary_persona: Shobha Toravi relishes spicy Mangalorean fare—neer dosa with fish curry, coconut rice, and sweet Mysore pak—preferring to savor them at local eateries, and occasionally experiments with fresh basil and curry leaves in simple home‑cooked dishes, though they rarely cook elaborate meals.
payload.persona: Shobha Toravi is a creatively restless tailor who dreams big, worries often, and juggles artistry with the daily grind of a modest shop.
payload.cultural_background: Shobha Toravi is a Hindu woman from the urban centre of Dakshina Kannada, Karnataka, a coastal region known for its blend of Kannada, Tulu and Konkani cultures. Although her family primarily follows the customs of mainland Karnataka—celebrating Ugadi, Deepavali, and local temple festivals—her day‑to‑day life is also shaped by the cosmopolitan atmosphere of Mangalore, where Hindi and English are common lingua‑francas for trade. Growing up in a modest household with limited formal schooling, she absorbed cultural knowledge through community gatherings, family rituals, and the visual richness of regional textiles and artisanal crafts. Her imaginative nature draws her to the colourful masquerade of local festivals and the abstract motifs that appear in coastal folk art.
payload.linguistic_background: Shobha's first language is English; she learned it informally through daily interactions with customers and neighbours. Despite having only below‑primary formal schooling, she can converse fluently, understand basic written English, and uses it to read garment labels, pattern guides and mobile messages. Her second language is Hindi, which she speaks comfortably to a wider Indian clientele and reads simple Hindi scripts such as market signs and devotional pamphlets. She has limited reading and writing proficiency in both languages due to her schooling level, but compensates with strong visual and practical skills.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Shobha is skilled in tailoring and dressmaking, with a keen eye for intricate embroidery and fabric selection. She can draft simple patterns, take precise measurements, and execute custom fittings. Her work includes client consultation, colour coordination, and adapting traditional motifs into contemporary designs. She also manages the day‑to‑day operations of her modest tailoring business, handling budgeting, supplies procurement, and improvisational problem‑solving (jugaad) when faced with material constraints.

payload.hobbies_and_interests: Shobha enjoys sketching abstract designs, experimenting with new fabric patterns, and listening to classical Hindustani music while she works. She often attends local craft fairs and folk dance performances for inspiration. In her leisure time, she likes taking walks along the Mangalore beach, gardening with native herbs, reading devotional stories, and watching classic Indian cinema. Occasionally she explores simple DIY home décor projects that combine her love for creativity and practicality.

Building a strong reputation for artistic excellence and achieving financial stability are key goals. Additionally, she seeks to improve her organisational habits and stress‑management strategies to sustain the business long‑term.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

payload.culinary_persona: Shobha Toravi relishes spicy Mangalorean fare—neer dosa with fish curry, coconut rice, and sweet Mysore pak—preferring to savor them at local eateries, and occasionally experiments with fresh basil and curry leaves in simple home‑cooked dishes, though they rarely cook elaborate meals.
payload.persona: Shobha Toravi is a creatively restless tailor who dreams big, worries often, and juggles artistry with the daily grind of a modest shop.
payload.cultural_background: Shobha Toravi is a Hindu woman from the urban centre of Dakshina Kannada, Karnataka, a coastal region known for its blend of Kannada, Tulu and Konkani cultures. Although her family primarily follows the customs of mainland Karnataka—celebrating Ugadi, Deepavali, and local temple festivals—her day‑to‑day life is also shaped by the cosmopolitan atmosphere of Mangalore, where Hindi and English are common lingua‑francas for trade. Growing up in a modest household with limited formal schooling, she absorbed cultural knowledge through community gatherings, family rituals, and the visual richness of regional textiles and artisanal crafts. Her imaginative nature draws her to the colourful masquerade of local festivals and the abstract motifs that appear in coastal folk art.
payload.linguistic_background: Shobha's first language is English; she learned it informally through daily interactions with customers and neighbours. Despite having only below‑primary formal schooling, she can converse fluently, understand basic written English, and uses it to read garment labels, pattern guides and mobile messages. Her second language is Hindi, which she speaks comfortably to a wider Indian clientele and reads simple Hindi scripts such as market signs and devotional pamphlets. She has limited reading and writing proficiency in both languages due to her schooling level, but compensates with strong visual and practical skills.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Shobha is skilled in tailoring and dressmaking, with a keen eye for intricate embroidery and fabric selection. She can draft simple patterns, take precise measurements, and execute custom fittings. Her work includes client consultation, colour coordination, and adapting traditional motifs into contemporary designs. She also manages the day‑to‑day operations of her modest tailoring business, handling budgeting, supplies procurement, and improvisational problem‑solving (jugaad) when faced with material constraints.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Tailoring', 'Dressmaking', 'Embroidery', 'Fabric Selection', 'Pattern Drafting', 'Custom Fitting', 'Client Consultation', 'Small Business Management', 'Creative Problem Solving (Jugaad)']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Shobha enjoys sketching abstract designs, experimenting with new fabric patterns, and listening to classical Hindustani music while she works. She often attends local craft fairs and folk dance performances for inspiration. In her leisure time, she likes taking walks along the Mangalore beach, gardening with native herbs, reading devotional stories, and watching classic Indian cinema. Occasionally she explores simple DIY home décor projects that combine her love for creativity and practicality.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Sketching', 'Experimenting with Fabric Patterns', 'Listening to Classical Hindustani Music', 'Attending Local Craft Fairs', 'Watching Folk Dance Performances', 'Beach Walks', 'Gardening with Native Herbs', 'Reading Devotional Stories', 'Watching Classic Indian Cinema', 'DIY Home Décor Projects']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Shobha aims to grow her modest tailoring unit into a recognized boutique that specializes in bespoke wedding and festival wear, blending contemporary aesthetics with traditional motifs. She plans to master advanced embroidery techniques, source eco‑friendly fabrics, and mentor a junior apprentice to ensure continuity. Building a strong reputation for artistic excellence and achieving financial stability are key goals. Additionally, she seeks to improve her organisational habits and stress‑management strategies to sustain the business long‑term.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 45-year-old female tailor from Dakshina Kannada, Karnataka, currently married, with below-primary education. She works in an urban tailoring shop, speaks English, and is married. Her lifestyle includes daily shop management and creative design work.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into English (code=en, script=Latin). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Karnataka, district=Dakshina Kannada.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Latin for English.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 45
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Tailors and Dress Makers, Other
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Karnataka
- district: Dakshina Kannada
- first_language: English
- clinical_persona_summary: A 45-year-old female tailor from Dakshina Kannada, Karnataka, currently married, with below-primary education. She works in an urban tailoring shop, speaks English, and is married. Her lifestyle includes daily shop management and creative design work.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Shanthi Shetty]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0315-001]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|shanthi.shetty@email.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Mangalore City Hospital]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Good morning Doctor. I've been having lower back pain for the past two weeks. It's worse when I sit at my sewing machine for long hours. I also feel very tired in the afternoons.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Nair]]: Good morning Shanthi. I understand your concern about the back pain and fatigue. Can you describe the pain more specifically? Is it sharp, dull, or burning?

Patient: It's mostly a dull ache, sometimes sharp when I stand up suddenly. The fatigue is making it hard to work. I'm a tailor, so I sit a lot.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Nair]]: I see. Given your occupation and symptoms, this sounds like mechanical low back pain with possible muscle fatigue. I recommend you take breaks every 30 minutes while working, use a cushion for lumbar support, and try gentle stretching exercises. Also, apply hot water bags for 15 minutes twice daily.

Patient: Thank you Doctor. Should I take any pain medication?

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Nair]]: Yes, you can take paracetamol 500mg when needed, but not more than 3 tablets in 24 hours. Also, start drinking more water and include calcium-rich foods in your diet. If the pain doesn't improve in a week, please visit our hospital for a physical examination.

Patient: Okay Doctor. I'll follow your advice. When should I come for the follow-up?

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Nair]]: Please schedule a follow-up in 7 days. If you develop fever, numbness in legs, or severe pain, come immediately to Mangalore City Hospital. Take care.

Traditional values of hospitality, joint family bonds, and the use of local crafts coexist with the growing presence of modern retail trade in her locality.

Although her formal schooling ended before primary level, she acquired basic literacy in Bengali through community adult‑learning sessions and on‑the‑job training. In her role as a sales supervisor she uses simple English phrases for interacting with suppliers and handling cash registers, but her proficiency remains conversational. She has no formal knowledge of Hindi and relies on occasional exposure through media and neighboring traders.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Amena brings strong expertise in retail sales supervision, including team leadership, inventory control, and customer relationship management. She is adept at organising product displays, handling cash transactions, and maintaining accurate sales records. Her negotiation skills help her secure favourable deals with local suppliers, and she confidently resolves customer queries.
