  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ER Triage & Nursing Notes for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kashmiri (code=ks, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Srinagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Kashmiri.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once in the arrival/RTA/ambulance line. Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 19
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Srinagar
- first_language: Kashmiri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 19‑year‑old male from Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir, who is never married and has no formal occupation, reports higher secondary education and is fluent in Kashmiri and functional English. He lives in an urban setting and works as a visual arts enthusiast, blending sketching, photography, and digital storytelling. His lifestyle includes occasional cricket and jogging, with a preference for casual social activities.

Document format guide for ER Triage & Nursing Notes (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|...]]
Arrived by ambulance / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|...]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Vitals / acuity: <short notes>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
ایمرجنسی ٹریاج — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SKIMS Soura]] ملاقات [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0815-001]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Imran Ahmad]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Male]] وارڈ [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] بید [[BED_NUMBER|07]]
ایمبولینس ذٔریعہ آمُت [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|JK01AB5678]]
رشتہ دار [[RELATIVE_NAME|Mohammad Yousuf]] فون [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419234567]] ڈاکٹر [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Farooq Ahmad]]
وائٹلز / حالت: ہوشس منٛز تہٕ 3 طرفہٕ باخبر، BP 120/80، HR 78، RR 16، Temp 36.8°C، SpO2 98% کمرے کی ہوا پر۔ مریض چھُ پریشان نظر یوان نفسیاتی حرکتی اشتعال سۭتۍ۔

نرسنگ تشخیص: مریض چھُ حالیہ تعلیمی دباؤ پتہٕ شدید پریشانی ہُنٛد واقعہ پیش کران۔ مریض چھُ پچھلے 3 دۄہن پؠٹھ سۄننس منٛز مشکل رپورٹ کران، مستقبل کیریئر کے امکانات کے بارے میں مداخلت خیالات، تہٕ امید کھو دینے کے احساسات۔ خودکشی کے خیالات یا خود کو نقصان پہنچانے کا ارادہ رپورٹ نہیں کیا گیا۔ مریض منشیات کے استعمال یا حالیہ دواؤں میں تبدیلیوں سے انکار کرتا ہے۔ خاندان رپورٹ کرتا ہے کہ مریض سماجی سرگرمیوں سے الگ ہو رہا ہے اور کمرے میں اکیلے زیادہ وقت گزار رہا ہے۔

مداخلت: ایک ایک مشاہدہ شروع کیا گیا، پرسکون ماحول فراہم کیا گیا، پریشانی کے لیے PRN لورازپیم 1mg PO دیا گیا۔ مریض کو مقابلہ کرنے کی حکمت عملیوں کے بارے میں تعلیم دی گئی اور نفسیاتی مشاورت کے لیے شیڈول کیا گیا۔ خاندان کو معاون دیکھ بھال کے بارے میں مشورہ دیا گیا اور انتباہی علامات کی نگرانی کے لیے۔
payload.generated_text_en:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SKIMS Soura]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0815-001]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Imran Ahmad]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|07]]
Arrived by ambulance vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|JK01AB5678]]
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|Mohammad Yousuf]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419234567]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Farooq Ahmad]]
Vitals / acuity: Alert and oriented x3, BP 120/80, HR 78, RR 16, Temp 36.8°C, SpO2 98% on room air. Patient appears anxious with psychomotor agitation.

Nursing assessment: Patient presents with acute anxiety episode following recent academic stress. Reports difficulty sleeping for past 3 days, intrusive thoughts about future career prospects, and feelings of hopelessness. No reported suicidal ideation or self-harm intent. Patient denies substance use or recent medication changes. Family reports patient has been withdrawing from social activities and spending excessive time alone in room.

Interventions: Initiated one-on-one observation, provided quiet environment, administered PRN lorazepam 1mg PO for anxiety. Patient educated on coping strategies and scheduled for psychiatry consultation. Family counseled on supportive care and monitoring for warning signs.

payload.professional_persona: Zainab Khatun is an experienced community caretaker who manages a large rural household, prepares traditional local cuisine, weaves shawls, and mediates neighbourhood disputes, and they apply their practical mindset, compassionate nature and balanced curiosity to innovate within folk traditions.
payload.linguistic_persona: Zainab Khatun communicates fluently in the regional language of their village, can converse in the local Hindi dialect, and memorises Arabic verses for prayers, though they cannot read or write in any script.
payload.sports_persona: Zainab Khatun enjoys watching Jamshedpur FC football matches on TV, attends local kabaddi tournaments, and maintains personal fitness through daily morning walks and gentle yoga, and they occasionally join a senior women's walking club that meets at the village school ground.
payload.arts_persona: Zainab Khatun immerses themselves in the regional folk song repertoire, especially the soulful renditions of the late Lalu Mishra, relishes the poetry of local bard Bhola Bhiya, and delights in classic Hindi cinema songs of Lata Mangeshkar and Kishore Kumar, and they often hum these tunes while weaving shawls.
payload.travel_persona: Zainab Khatun prefers short, purpose-driven trips such as travelling to the nearest town market in Hazaribagh for fresh produce, visiting relatives in Ranchi during festive seasons, and occasionally joining organized pilgrim tours to the Shravan Temple in Deoghar, and they balance modest travel budgets with a love for family reunions.
payload.culinary_persona: Zainab Khatun excels at preparing traditional local dishes such as mutton keema, spicy dal with mustard seed, sewai sweet flavored with cardamom and saffron, and seasonal vegetable bhaji from their kitchen garden, preferring fresh, locally sourced ingredients while occasionally experimenting with a pinch of modern spices, and they take great pride in serving these meals to family and guests.
payload.persona: Zainab Khatun combines a lifelong devotion to community caretaking with a pragmatic curiosity, weaving stories and shawls while juggling budgeting, health rituals, and occasional sweet indulgences, and they are guided by a steady, compassionate temperament.
payload.cultural_background: Zainab Khatun belongs to a Maithili‑speaking Muslim community that has lived for generations in the rural hinterland of Hazaribagh, Jharkhand. The community blends the linguistic and folkloric traditions of the Maithili culture—such as panche dhun folk songs, seasonal fairs, and oral storytelling—with Islamic religious practices like daily prayers, Ramadan fasting, and Eid celebrations. Village life is organised around joint families, agricultural cycles, and a strong sense of neighbourly reciprocity, which have shaped Zainab’s worldview and daily routine.
payload.linguistic_background: Zainab grew up speaking Maithili as her mother tongue and uses it as her primary mode of communication at home and in the village. She can understand and converse in spoken Hindi, especially the local dialect, but due to her lack of formal schooling she cannot read or write in any script. For religious purposes she has learned to recite Arabic verses from memory and can follow the Urdu script of the Qur'an with the help of family members. Her oral proficiency enables her to preserve and transmit folk stories and culinary knowledge across generations.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Zainab’s expertise lies in managing a large rural household, preparing traditional Maithili Muslim cuisine, preserving oral folklore, providing informal caregiving and community support, performing basic arithmetic for budgeting, weaving handicrafts, and mediating disputes within the neighbourhood. Her practical mindset and compassionate nature allow her to apply these skills with reliability and flexibility.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Traditional Maithili Muslim cooking", "Oral folklore preservation", "Community caregiving", "Basic arithmetic budgeting", "Handicraft weaving", "Neighbourhood dispute mediation"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Zainab enjoys listening to Maithili folk songs, weaving colorful shawls, tending a small kitchen garden of seasonal vegetables, reciting du’a and Qur’anic verses from memory, sharing stories with grandchildren, and participating in women’s self‑help groups that discuss health, nutrition, and religious topics.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Maithili folk music", "Weaving colorful shawls", "Tending a kitchen garden", "Reciting du'a and Qur'anic verses from memory", "Storytelling with grandchildren", "Participating in women’s self‑help groups"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Even at 65, Zainab aspires to remain an active pillar of her community. She wishes to mentor younger women in traditional cooking and weaving, document oral stories for future generations, and organize health and hygiene awareness camps for the villagers. She also hopes to support her grandchildren’s education through informal tutoring and to continue her voluntary work at the mosque, ensuring that the values of compassion and harmony are passed on.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 65-year-old female from Jharkhand, Hazaribagh, who is widowed and retired, has limited formal education and speaks Maithili at home. She lives in a rural area and manages household responsibilities, including traditional cooking and community caretaking. Her lifestyle includes daily walks and occasional participation in local senior women's walking groups.

- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;

GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Homemaker]] | MRN: [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sushila Devi]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9431023456]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-2024-008]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Hazaribagh]]
Chief complaint: Persistent cough for 3 months with weight loss and evening fever

History of present illness: Patient presents with productive cough for approximately 90 days, initially dry then became productive with yellowish sputum. Reports low-grade evening fever for the past 2 months. Experiencing progressive weight loss of approximately 8 kg over 3 months. Associated symptoms include mild chest discomfort and fatigue.

Past medical history: Hypertension diagnosed 5 years ago, currently on irregular medication. No known diabetes. No previous history of tuberculosis.

Physical examination:
- General: Thin, cachectic appearance, conscious and oriented
- Vital signs: BP 140/90 mmHg, Pulse 92/min, Temperature 99.2°F, Respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 94% on room air
- Chest: Decreased breath sounds in right upper zone, few crepitations present

Investigations:

- Chest X-ray: Right upper lobe infiltrate with cavitation
- Hemoglobin: 9.8 g/dL
- Blood sugar (fasting): 110 mg/dL
- ESR: 85 mm/hr

Diagnosis:
1. Pulmonary Tuberculosis (Rifampicin-sensitive)
2. Hypertension
3. Nutritional deficiency anemia

Treatment plan:
- Anti-tubercular therapy: Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol for intensive phase (2 months)
- Continuation phase: Isoniazid and Rifampicin for 4 months
- Antihypertensive medication: Amlodipine 5mg daily
- Nutritional support: High-protein diet, iron supplements
- Follow-up: Weekly review for first month, then monthly

Counseling: Patient advised about importance of completing full course of treatment, regular medication for hypertension, and dietary modifications. Relative instructed to ensure treatment compliance.

Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kashmiri (code=ks, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Srinagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Kashmiri.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 28
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Keypunch Operator
- education_level: Technical diploma or certificate not equal to degree
- zone: Urban
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Srinagar
- first_language: Kashmiri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 28-year-old male from Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir, currently married, works as a keypunch operator with a technical diploma and resides in an urban zone. His first language is Kashmiri, and he has functional English proficiency. He reports occasional anxiety and a habit of impulse art-supply purchases, while engaging in quiet hobbies like poetry and Sufi music.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

جسمانی معائنہٕ:

payload.generated_text_en:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sher-i-Kashmir Institute of Medical Sciences]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|SKIMS-SRI-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Mohammad Yousuf]] | DOB [[DOB|1996-05-15]] | Age: [[AGE|28]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Male]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Keypunch Operator]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-SKIMS-2024-0876]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Abdul Rashid]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Fatima Begum]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419012345]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|REG-2024-0156]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Srinagar]]

Chief complaint: Persistent cough for 3 weeks with occasional low-grade fever and night sweats, associated with increased anxiety episodes

History of present illness: Patient reports productive cough with yellowish sputum, worse in early mornings. Episodes of low-grade fever (99-100°F) and drenching night sweats for past 3 weeks. Reports increased anxiety and restlessness, particularly at night. No hemoptysis noted. Appetite decreased, weight loss of 3 kg over 2 months.

Past medical history: Previously healthy. No known comorbidities. Occasional anxiety episodes for 1 year, managed with occasional paracetamol.

Physical examination:
- General: Thin build, anxious appearance, mildly pale
- Respiratory: Chest clear bilaterally, no wheezes or rhonchi
- Cardiovascular: Regular heart sounds, no murmurs
- Abdomen: Soft, non-tender

Investigations ordered:
- Sputum for AFB smear and culture
- Chest X-ray PA view
- Complete blood count
- ESR
- Blood sugar fasting and postprandial
- Liver function tests

Diagnosis: Suspected pulmonary tuberculosis with anxiety

Treatment plan:
- Start anti-tubercular therapy (ATT) as per RNTCP guidelines
- Prescribe anxiolytic medication for anxiety management
- Nutritional counseling
- Follow-up in 2 weeks for review of investigations and treatment response

Patient counseled about TB transmission, treatment adherence, and importance of completing full course of ATT.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kashmiri (code=ks, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Srinagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Kashmiri.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 28
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Keypunch Operator
- education_level: Technical diploma or certificate not equal to degree
- zone: Urban
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Srinagar
- first_language: Kashmiri
- clinical_persona_summary: Jammer Shamar, a 28-year-old male, is a keypunch operator with a technical diploma, currently married and residing in Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir. His primary language is Kashmiri, and he works in an urban setting. He is detail-oriented and manages anxiety, with a habit of occasional impulse art-supply purchases.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
--- سیشن ---
مریض [[PATIENT_NAME|Jammer Shamar]] [[AGE|28]] [[GENDER|Male]]
اپوائنٹمنٹ [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0815-001]] پورٹل [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
کلائنٹ IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] ڈیوائس IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] فون [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
ای میل [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|jammer.shamar@example.com]] ہسپتال [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SMHS Hospital Srinagar]]
--- چیٹ ---
مریض: سلام ڈاکٹر صاحب، بہٕ چھس پچھلے شامہٕ پؠٹھ تپَس منٛز۔ درجہ حرارت تقریباً 101°F، جِسمَس منٛز دۆد، تہٕ ہلکہٕ کھۆکھ۔ بہٕ چھس اکھ کی پنچ آپریٹر تہٕ میزس پؠٹھ بہنہٕ سۭتۍ چھُ یہِ مزیٖد خراب گژھان۔

ڈاکٹر [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: سلام جمر، بہٕ چھس تُہنٛز پریشانی سمجھان۔ تپ، جِسمَس منٛز دۆد تہٕ کھۆکھ چھُ وایرل اِنفیکشن ہنٛز نِشاندہی کران۔ کیا تُہہِ چھِ کینٛہہ دوایاں ہیتمژٕ، مِثلاً paracetamol؟

مریض: آں ڈاکٹر، مےٚ ہیت 650mg paracetamol زِ لٹہِ مگر تپ چھُ وُنی تہِ زیٛادٕ۔ مزیٖد، مےٚ چھُ کَلَس منٛز دۆد تہٕ گَردَنَس منٛز دۆد۔ مگر ساہ ہینہٕ منٛز کینٛہہ تکیٖف چھُ نہٕ۔

ڈاکٹر [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: یہِ چھُ جان زِ تُہہِ چھِ ساہ ہینہٕ پؠٹھ نظر تھاوان۔ paracetamol 650mg پرٛٮ۪تھ 6 گھنٹن پؠٹھ جاری تھٲوِیو۔ دۄہس منٛز 2-3 لٹہِ بھاپ ہینہٕ ہنٛز عمل شٲمل کٔرِیو۔ واریاہ گرم آب چیو۔

مریض: کیا مےٚ پزِ ہسپتال یُن یا کیا یہِ گھرس منٛز سنبھالنہٕ یِیہِ؟ بہٕ چھس پگاہ کٲمِہ پؠٹھ غٲر حاضر روزنہٕ خٲطرٕ پریشان۔

ڈاکٹر [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: تُہنٛدٮ۪ن علامتن ہنٛدِ بنیادس پؠٹھ، گھرس منٛز علاج چھُ موزوں۔ جان آرام کٔرِیو، زیٛادٕ کھین کھینہٕ نش بچِیو۔ اگر تپ 103°F پؠٹھ زیٛادٕ گژھِ یا ساہ ہینہٕ منٛز تکیٖف گژھِ، توتہِ سمہس ایمرجنسی منٛز فوراً گژھِیو۔

مریض: شکریہ ڈاکٹر۔ اکھ مزیٖد سوال - کیا بہٕ ہٮ۪کہٕ کینٛہہ کھۆکھ ہنٛز دوا ہیتھ؟

ڈاکٹر [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: وُنی خٲطرٕ کھۆکھ ہنٛز دوا نش بچِیو۔ اگر کھۆکھ 3 دۄہن پؠٹھ زیٛادٕ روزِ، توتہِ ہٮ۪کو أسۍ اَتھ پؠٹھ غور کٔرتھ۔ جمر، جَلدی ٹھیک گژھِیو۔

مریض: کرہ بہٕ ڈاکٹر۔ تُہنٛزِ مددِ خٲطرٕ شکریہ۔
payload.generated_text_en:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Jammer Shamar]] [[AGE|28]] [[GENDER|Male]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0815-001]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|jammer.shamar@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SMHS Hospital Srinagar]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Namaste doctor saheb, I've been having fever since yesterday evening. Temperature around 101°F, body ache, and mild cough. I work as a keypunch operator and sitting at desk makes it worse.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: Hello Jammer, I understand your concern. Fever with body ache and cough suggests viral infection. Have you taken any medications like paracetamol?

Patient: Yes doctor, took 650mg paracetamol twice but fever still high. Also feeling headache and throat pain. No breathing difficulty though.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: Good that you're monitoring breathing. Continue paracetamol 650mg every 6 hours. Add steam inhalation 2-3 times daily. Drink plenty of warm fluids.

Patient: Should I come to hospital or is this manageable at home? I'm worried about missing work tomorrow.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: Based on your symptoms, home management is appropriate. Rest well, avoid heavy meals. If fever exceeds 103°F or breathing becomes difficult, visit SMHS Emergency immediately.

Patient: Thank you doctor. One more question - can I take any cough syrup?

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: Avoid cough syrup for now. If cough persists beyond 3 days, we can consider it. Get well soon Jammer.

Patient: Will do doctor. Thanks for your help.

payload.professional_persona: Devanti Kumari, a 23‑year‑old jewellery and engraving supervisor, leverages her hands‑on metal‑working expertise, meticulous scheduling, and market‑savvy eye while channeling her structured, sociable nature into keeping her artisan team harmonious, though her occasional anxiety makes her double‑check every intricate design.
payload.linguistic_persona: Devanti Kumari speaks Maithili fluently as her mother tongue, comprehends spoken Hindi through daily radio and community interactions, but remains illiterate in both languages and has no knowledge of English.
payload.sports_persona: Devanti Kumari enjoys playing carrom with friends, follows the Patna Pirates in the Pro Kabaddi League, and tunes into India’s cricket matches while joining seasonal village cricket games whenever she finds time.
payload.arts_persona: Devanti Kumari delights in singing Maithili folk songs and bhajans, especially the renditions of Sharda Sinha, regularly watches Madhubani performances by artists like Ganga Devi at local melas, and often quotes verses from Lata Mangeshkar’s classics during gatherings.
payload.travel_persona: Devanti Kumari prefers short trips to nearby towns such as Darbhanga for festive markets and Patna for jewellery supplies, while dreaming of a pilgrimage to Varanasi and a cultural tour of Delhi’s historic sites when finances allow.
payload.culinary_persona: Devanti Kumari loves preparing litti‑chokha with sattu stuffing, baking sweet peda with khoya, and savoring seasonal mangoes, though she sticks to traditional recipes rather than experimenting with fusion cooking.
payload.persona: Devanti Kumari is a diligent 23‑year‑old jewellery supervisor whose structured work ethic and vibrant social spirit are tempered by frequent anxiety and a dream of leading her own craft studio.
payload.cultural_background: Devanti comes from a Maithili‑speaking family in the rural hinterland of Saharsa, Bihar. Her upbringing is rooted in the agrarian culture of the Mithila region, where festivals such as Chhath, Jitiya and Saawan are celebrated with elaborate rituals. The local community places high value on family cohesion, respect for elders, and the preservation of traditional arts like Madhubani painting and handmade jewellery. Growing up in a joint‑family setting, she learned the importance of mutual support, hospitality and communal gatherings.
payload.linguistic_background: Devanti is a native speaker of Maithili and uses it as her primary means of communication at home and in the community. Although she cannot read or write due to her illiteracy, she has acquired basic comprehension of spoken Hindi through daily interactions and media such as radio. She does not have proficiency in English or any other language.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Devanti has developed strong supervisory skills in the jewellery and precision metal‑working sector. She coordinates daily production schedules, ensures that engraving patterns meet design specifications, and conducts quality checks on finished pieces. Her hands‑on experience with tools such as hand‑drills, gravers and polishing machines gives her practical expertise in metal shaping and fine detailing. Additionally, she manages a small team of artisans, handling labour allocation, conflict resolution and on‑the‑spot training. Her keen eye for market trends helps her adapt designs to customer preferences in the local market.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Supervision of jewellery production", "Engraving techniques", "Metal shaping and polishing", "Quality control", "Team management", "Market trend awareness"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Devanti enjoys singing traditional Maithili folk songs and bhajans during temple festivities and family gatherings. She frequently participates in local melas, where she likes watching cultural performances and browsing handmade crafts. Cooking regional dishes such as litti‑chokha and preparing sweets for festivals is a cherished pastime. She also relaxes by listening to the village radio, playing carrom with friends, and practising simple breathing exercises to manage her occasional anxiety.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Singing Maithili folk songs", "Attending local melas", "Cooking traditional dishes", "Listening to the village radio", "Playing carrom", "Practicing breathing exercises"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Devanti aspires to open her own modest jewellery and engraving studio within the next three to five years, allowing her to generate a stable income for her family and create employment opportunities for other women in her village. She also wishes to acquire basic literacy skills so she can handle bookkeeping, read design catalogs and communicate with suppliers more effectively. In the longer term, she hopes to become a respected mentor in the local craft community, preserving traditional Maithili motifs while integrating modern design trends.
payload.occupation: Supervisor, Jewellery, Engraving and Precision Metal Workers
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Devanti Kumari is a 23‑year‑old female jewellery supervisor from rural Saharsa, Bihar, who is currently married and has an illiterate education level. payload.system_prompt:

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Maithili (code=mai, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Saharsa.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Maithili.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 23
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Supervisor, Jewellery, Engraving and Precision Metal Workers
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Saharsa
- first_language: Maithili
- clinical_persona_summary: Devanti Kumari is a 23‑year‑old female jewellery supervisor from rural Saharsa, Bihar, who is currently married and has an illiterate education level. She speaks Maithili at home and has basic comprehension of spoken Hindi. Her structured work ethic is tempered by anxiety, and she seeks to lead her own craft studio.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.professional_persona: Lalita Tirvadekar, a mono line operator in a rural telecom network, combines sharp manual dexterity and an ear for line quality with vivid storytelling to explain technical steps; they keep a relaxed, flexible approach that sometimes makes their schedule a bit disorganized.

payload.arts_persona: Lalita Tirvadekar, an enthusiastic creator of abstract folk motifs on fabric and paper, spends evenings immersed in the ragas of Pandit Bhimsen Joshi and the tabla rhythms of Ustad Zakir Hussain, while practicing lively Lavani steps during village celebrations, and draws inspiration from the vivid colors of local Kalamkari artisans.
payload.travel_persona: Lalita Tirvadekar, who prefers spontaneous, budget‑friendly getaways, has explored the misty hills of Mahabaleshwar, the sandy beaches of Goa, and the ancient forts of Raigad; they often travel with a small group of friends during festival holidays to combine cultural immersion with relaxed sightseeing.
payload.culinary_persona: Lalita Tirvadekar, who delights in the sweet aroma of fresh coconut in dishes like sol kadhi, loves the tangy heat of misal pav and the festive sweetness of modak, often prepares simple vangi bhaji at home using brinjal and tamarind, yet prefers to savor street‑food specialties such as vada pav and bhel puri during village fairs.
payload.persona: Lalita Tirvadekar is a 34‑year‑old mono‑line operator who weaves technical precision with vibrant folk art, thrives on community rituals yet juggles anxiety‑driven moods, lives on disciplined savings but occasionally splurges on colorful craft supplies; they balance imagination with a relaxed, sometimes disorganized lifestyle.
payload.cultural_background: Lalita Tirvadekar comes from a Konkani‑speaking Hindu community that settled in the rural hinterland of Jalgaon, Maharashtra. Her family maintains a blend of coastal Konkani customs—such as devotion to Ganesh and sharing of coconut‑based festive foods—and the agrarian traditions of interior Maharashtra, like local fairs (shashti), collective farming rituals and village temple gatherings. This cultural mix shapes her worldview, fostering a respect for community harmony and a love for folk arts.
payload.linguistic_background: Lalita grew up speaking Konkani at home and Marathi in the village and school setting. Though she is illiterate and cannot read or write in any language, she has strong oral communication skills. She can understand spoken Hindi due to everyday interactions and can follow work instructions in Konkani and Marathi. Her fluency lets her convey ideas creatively through storytelling and oral explanations, which aligns with her imaginative nature.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Lalita works as a mono line operator, handling installation, maintenance, and troubleshooting of single‑wire telephone lines in her rural area. She possesses manual dexterity, an ear for line quality, and the ability to interpret oral work instructions. Her job requires clear communication with villagers, so she has strong interpersonal and conflict‑resolution skills. Additionally, her creative side finds expression in folk art, embroidery, and improvisational storytelling, which she often uses to convey information in a memorable way.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['mono line operation', 'basic electrical troubleshooting', 'manual dexterity', 'oral instruction following', 'community liaison', 'folk art creation', 'embroidery', 'conflict mediation']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Lalita enjoys creating abstract paintings and colorful folk motifs on fabric and paper. She spends evenings listening to classical Hindustani music and practicing the lively Lavani dance steps during village celebrations. Gardening, especially growing seasonal vegetables and marigolds, gives her a sense of calm. She loves oral storytelling, sharing local legends with children, and attending weekly village fairs where she explores new crafts and ideas.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['abstract painting', 'folk motif creation', 'listening to Hindustani classical music', 'practicing Lavani dance', 'gardening', 'oral storytelling', 'attending village fairs', 'exploring new crafts']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Lalita aspires to advance from being a mono line operator to a supervisory role within the telecom department, where she can mentor other workers. She plans to enroll in a government adult‑literacy program to gain basic reading and writing skills, which will enable her to handle records independently. In the longer term, she dreams of turning her talent for folk art and embroidery into a small home‑based handicraft business, providing supplemental income for her family and promoting cultural heritage.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Lalita Tirvadekar is a 34‑year‑old female from a Konkani‑speaking Hindu background in rural Jalgaon, Maharashtra, currently married. She works as a mono‑line operator, has an illiterate education, and lives in a rural zone. Her lifestyle includes community rituals, anxiety‑driven moods, and disciplined savings with occasional splurges. She is fluent in Konkani and Marathi, has strong oral communication, and understands spoken Hindi.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):

She works as a mono‑line operator, has an illiterate education, and lives in a rural zone. Her lifestyle includes community rituals, anxiety‑driven moods, and disciplined savings with occasional splurges. She is fluent in Konkani and Marathi, has strong oral communication, and understands spoken Hindi.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):

Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:

- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]

- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

सद्याच्या दुयेंसाचो इतिहास: रुग्ण सांगता की कपाळांत तकली दुखपाची हळूहळू सुरवात जाली, जें मंद दुखप अशें सांगलां आनी 6/10 तीव्रतेचें मानलां. घरा नोंद जाल्लो जोर सुमार 100.4°F तापमाना वांगडा. प्रकाश भंय वा ध्वनी भंय नोंद जावंक ना. वांगडा सगळ्या कुडींत दूख आनी थोडो थकवो आसा. उलटी, मळमळ वा मानेची कडकपणा ना.

तपासणी: CBC सौम्य ल्युकोसाइटोसिस वांगडा न्यूट्रोफिलिया दाखयता. Widal चाचणी नकारात्मक. COVID-19 खातीर Rapid antigen चाचणी नकारात्मक.

उपचार: जोर आनी तकली दुखपा खातीर Paracetamol 500mg TDS. विश्रांती, पुरो उदक पियेंवात आनी हलको आहार घेवपाचो सल्ला. जर लक्षणां तशींच उरलीं तर 3 दिसांनी परत तपासणी.

OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Jalgaon]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-JLG-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Lalita Tirvadekar]] | DOB [[DOB|1990-05-15]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Mono Operator]] | MRN: [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Deshmukh]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Tirvadekar]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Jalgaon]]
Chief complaint: Patient presents with persistent headache and mild fever for 3 days, associated with body ache and fatigue.

History of present illness: Patient reports gradual onset of frontal headache, described as dull aching, rated 6/10 in severity. Fever noted at home with temperature around 100.4°F. No photophobia or phonophobia reported. Associated with generalized body ache and mild fatigue. No nausea, vomiting, or neck stiffness.

Examination: Vitals - BP 120/80 mmHg, Pulse 88/min, Temperature 100.2°F, Respiratory rate 18/min. General - Patient appears mildly fatigued, no acute distress. Head - Normocephalic, no tenderness. CVS - Regular heart sounds, no murmurs. Respiratory - Clear breath sounds bilaterally. Abdomen - Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly. Neurological - Alert and oriented, no focal deficits.

Investigations: CBC shows mild leukocytosis with neutrophilia. Widal test negative. Rapid antigen test for COVID-19 negative.

Diagnosis: Viral fever with tension-type headache.

Treatment: Paracetamol 500mg TDS for fever and headache. Advise rest, adequate hydration, and light diet. Follow-up in 3 days if symptoms persist.

Advice: Avoid strenuous activity, maintain hydration, take medications as prescribed. Return immediately if high-grade fever develops or severe headache persists.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.

Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:

GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags

BAD → instruction_drift:

  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:

GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
