payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Maithili (code=mai, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jharkhand, district=Hazaribagh.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Maithili.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 65
- marital_status: Widowed
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Jharkhand
- district: Hazaribagh
- first_language: Maithili
- clinical_persona_summary: A 65-year-old female from Jharkhand, Hazaribagh district, who is widowed and retired, speaks Maithili at home and has limited formal education. She lives in a rural area and manages household responsibilities, with a lifestyle that includes traditional cooking and community engagement. Her health behaviors are shaped by local customs, with limited access to formal healthcare.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
नुस्खा — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Shri Krishna Hospital, Hazaribagh]]
मरीज [[PATIENT_NAME|Sita Devi]], [[AGE|65]] / [[GENDER|Female]], MRN [[MRN|MRN-JH-2024-001]]
[[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|Village Barkagaon, Hazaribagh, Jharkhand]]
[[DISTRICT|Hazaribagh]]
डाक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajiv Kumar]]
ABHA [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
मरीज [[PATIENT_ID|PID-JH-65-001]]
संपर्क [[PHONE_NUMBER|9431056789]]
Rx: ऑपरेशनक बादक देखभाल laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
1. Tab. Paracetamol 500mg, एक गोली दिनमे दू बेर भोजनक बाद पीड़ा प्रबंधनक लेल।
2. Cap. Cefixime 200mg, एक कैप्सूल दिनमे एक बेर 5 दिनक लेल।
निर्देश: घरमे आराम करू। कठिन गतिविधि सँ बचू। 7 दिनमे जाँचक लेल आउ।
आपातकालीन स्थितिमे, अस्पतालसँ संपर्क करू [[PHONE_NUMBER|9431056789]]।
payload.generated_text_en:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Shri Krishna Hospital, Hazaribagh]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Sita Devi]], [[AGE|65]] / [[GENDER|Female]], MRN [[MRN|MRN-JH-2024-001]]
[[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|Village Barkagaon, Hazaribagh, Jharkhand]]
[[DISTRICT|Hazaribagh]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajiv Kumar]]
ABHA [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
Patient [[PATIENT_ID|PID-JH-65-001]]
Contact [[PHONE_NUMBER|9431056789]]
Rx: Post-operative care following laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
1. Tab. Paracetamol 500mg, one tablet twice daily after food for pain management.
2. Cap. Cefixime 200mg, one capsule once daily for 5 days.
Instructions: Rest at home. Avoid strenuous activity. Return for follow-up in 7 days.
For emergencies, contact the hospital at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9431056789]].

payload.professional_persona: Sophia Devassykutty, a retired community culinary coordinator and church volunteer, they blend their mastery of traditional Kerala cuisine, simple bookkeeping, basic first‑aid, and informal counseling with a gentle, structured yet flexible approach that values harmony and prefers intimate gatherings over large events.
payload.linguistic_persona: Sophia Devassykutty, fluent in Malayalam and able to read and write it comfortably, they manage household accounts and correspond with relatives in Malayalam, while their English reading is limited to church newsletters and simple recipes, and they have no knowledge of Hindi or any other languages.
payload.sports_persona: Sophia Devassykutty, enjoys casual cricket matches with neighborhood friends and follows the Kerala Blasters in the Indian Super League, they participate in a local senior walking club that meets twice a week along the Thrissur Riverfront, preferring low‑impact activities over intense workouts.
payload.arts_persona: Sophia Devassykutty, a lover of Malayalam devotional music by K. J. Yesudas and classic cinema featuring Mohanlal and Adoor Gopalakrishnan, they regularly attend church choir rehearsals, enjoy reading O. N. V. Kurup’s poetry, and find comfort in watching timeless Malayalam films such as 'Nirmalyam' and 'Chemmeen'.
payload.travel_persona: Sophia Devassykutty, prefers low‑key, family‑oriented trips over grand tours, they enjoy short getaways to Kerala’s backwaters, Munnar’s tea estates, and Cochin’s historic Fort, while dreaming of a modest pilgrimage to Sri Lanka’s ancient temples, planning each journey with a simple itinerary and a modest budget.
payload.culinary_persona: Sophia Devassykutty, a master of traditional Kerala fare, they expertly prepare Sadhya with banana leaf presentation, aromatic fish curry using tamarind and coconut oil, fluffy appam with coconut milk, and spicy puttu with kadala, relishing the fragrant notes of curry leaves, mustard seeds, and fresh banana saplings from their balcony garden.
payload.persona: Sophia Devassykutty blends disciplined culinary tradition with a relaxed, slightly disorganized lifestyle, cherishing family, modest budgeting, and quiet devotion to service, while occasionally postponing bills and obsessively knitting scarves for loved ones.
payload.cultural_background: Sophia belongs to the Syrian Christian community of Thrissur, Kerala, a city known for its vibrant festivals and strong communal ties. Growing up in an urban middle‑class household, she was immersed in the unique blend of Kerala’s Malayali culture—its cuisine, music, and temple festivals—while practicing Christian traditions such as Christmas, Easter, and weekly Mass. The close‑knit neighbourhoods of Thrissur, with their emphasis on family, hospitality and community service, have shaped her outlook and daily routines.
payload.linguistic_background: Sophia's mother tongue is Malayalam, which she reads, writes and speaks fluently. Her primary education gave her functional literacy in Malayalam, allowing her to manage household accounts, read religious texts, and correspond with relatives through letters. She has limited English reading ability, primarily for church newsletters and simple recipes, but does not use English in daily conversation. She does not speak Hindi or any other language.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Sophia is highly skilled in traditional Kerala cuisine, especially preparing Sadhya, fish curries, appam and puttu. She manages household finances, keeping simple ledgers for monthly expenses. Her practical expertise includes basic first‑aid, home gardening of spices and banana plants, and embroidery/knitting for family gifts. She also coordinates church events, arranging meals and volunteers, and offers informal counseling to younger women in her community.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Kerala cooking (Sadhya, fish curry, appam, puttu)', 'Household financial management', 'Basic first aid', 'Home gardening (spices, banana plants)', 'Embroidery and knitting', 'Church event coordination', 'Informal counseling for community women']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Sophia enjoys cooking traditional Kerala dishes, especially for family gatherings and church feasts. She loves tending to her small balcony garden where she grows curry leaves, basil, and banana saplings. In her leisure time, she listens to Malayalam devotional music, watches classic Malayalam movies, reads light Malayalam poetry, and participates in the church choir on special occasions. She also relaxes by knitting scarves for relatives and taking gentle walks along the riverfront.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Cooking traditional Kerala dishes', 'Balcony gardening', 'Listening to Malayalam devotional music', 'Watching classic Malayalam movies', 'Reading Malayalam poetry', 'Participating in church choir', 'Knitting scarves', 'Walking along the riverfront']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Although retired, Sophia aims to preserve her family’s culinary heritage by documenting recipes in a handwritten cookbook to pass on to her grandchildren. She wishes to expand her role in the church by leading the women’s fellowship group, organizing health‑awareness sessions, and mentoring younger mothers. Additionally, she hopes to maintain good health through daily walks and a small vegetable garden, ensuring she remains an active, supportive presence in her family and community.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 56-year-old female, currently married, retired homemaker with primary education, living in an urban area of Thrissur, Kerala. Her first language is Malayalam, and she belongs to the Syrian Christian community. Clinically, she has a modest lifestyle with a focus on family and community service, and may have irregular health maintenance behaviors.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:

  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:

  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).

Language SLA:

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Kerala, district=Thrissur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Malayalam for Malayalam.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Her first language is Malayalam, and she belongs to the Syrian Christian community. Clinically, she has a modest lifestyle with a focus on family and community service, and may have irregular health maintenance behaviors.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:

- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]

- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Thrissur]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-KL-027]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Marykutty Thomas]] | DOB [[DOB|1968-07-22]] | Age: [[AGE|56]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Retired Homemaker]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0815-004]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Nair]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Thomas Thomas]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-KL-0089]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Thrissur]]
Chief complaint: Persistent cough for 3 weeks with weight loss and fever

History of present illness: Patient presents with productive cough, low-grade fever in evenings, and progressive weight loss of approximately 5 kg over the past month. She reports night sweats and mild dyspnea on exertion. Past medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus for 8 years. She is on regular antihypertensive and antidiabetic medications.

Examination findings: Temperature 37.8°C, BP 140/90 mmHg, Pulse 92/min, Respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 94% on room air. Chest examination reveals decreased breath sounds in right upper zone with occasional crackles. No clubbing noted.

Investigations: Sputum for AFB smear and GeneXpert ordered. HbA1c 8.2%, fasting blood sugar 145 mg/dL. Chest X-ray shows right upper lobe infiltrates with cavitation.

Diagnosis: Pulmonary Tuberculosis with Diabetes Mellitus and Hypertension

Treatment plan: Started on DOTS regimen for TB. Increased dosage of antidiabetic medications. Blood pressure medications adjusted. Patient counseled on treatment adherence and follow-up in 2 weeks.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.

NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Maithili.

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.

payload.generated_text_en:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Saharsa District Hospital]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Devanti Kumari]], [[AGE|23]] / [[GENDER|Female]], MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-SAH-001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajiv Kumar]]
Chief Complaint: Persistent cough for three weeks, low-grade evening fever, and weight loss.
History: Patient is a 23-year-old female, married, from rural Madhupur. Occupation: Jewellery Supervisor. Reports occasional smoking. No known comorbidities.
Examination: Mild pallor and cachexia. Chest auscultation reveals crackles in the right upper zone.
Investigations: Sputum for AFB smear and GeneXpert requested. HbA1c: 6.4% (Pre-diabetic range).
Diagnosis: Pulmonary Tuberculosis (New Case), Pre-diabetes.
Rx:
Intensive Phase (2 months):
Isoniazid 300mg once daily
Rifampicin 450mg once daily
Pyrazinamide 1500mg once daily
Ethambutol 800mg once daily
Continuation Phase (4 months):
Isoniazid 300mg once daily
Rifampicin 450mg once daily
Instructions: Take all medications on an empty stomach in the morning. Complete the full course of treatment. Do not stop even if you feel better. Return for monthly follow-up. ASHA worker [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Devi]] to provide adherence counseling.
For any queries, contact the hospital at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9431023456]].
Patient ID: [[PATIENT_ID|PID-2024-7890]]
ABHA ID: [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
Address: [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|Village: Madhupur, Post: Saur Bazar, Saharsa, Bihar - 852201]]
District: [[DISTRICT|Saharsa]]
Hospital ID: [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-SAH-001]]

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Malayalam (code=ml, script=Malayalam). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Kerala, district=Thiruvananthapuram.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Malayalam for Malayalam.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 32
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Labourer, Other
- education_level: Graduate & above
- zone: Rural
- state: Kerala
- district: Thiruvananthapuram
- first_language: Malayalam
- clinical_persona_summary: Megha John is a 32-year-old female labourer from Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala, currently married, with a graduate degree and a rural occupation. She speaks Malayalam and has functional English proficiency, and her lifestyle includes daily walks and occasional yoga. Her community-oriented background is rooted in rural Kerala culture.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Megha John]] [[AGE|32]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Nair]]
Procedure / findings: Patient presented with acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain for 2 days. On examination, tenderness at McBurney's point with rebound tenderness. Ultrasound showed inflamed appendix with diameter 8mm. Proceeded with laparoscopic appendectomy. Appendix was found to be perforated with purulent discharge. Appendix removed and sent for histopathology. Wound closed with sutures. Patient tolerated procedure well.
Post-operative plan: Monitor vitals, analgesics, antibiotics, NPO for 6 hours then clear liquids.

[[MRN|MRN-2024-0912-001]]
[[RELATIVE_NAME|John John]]
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-78945]]

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.

- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).

- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Malayalam (code=ml, script=Malayalam). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Kerala, district=Thrissur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Malayalam for Malayalam.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 56
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Kerala
- district: Thrissur
- first_language: Malayalam
- clinical_persona_summary: A 56-year-old female from Thrissur, Kerala, who is currently married and retired, has primary education and speaks Malayalam. She lives in an urban zone and is a homemaker. Clinically, she has a disciplined lifestyle with modest budgeting, occasional procrastination on bills, and a history of knitting. She is part of the Syrian Christian community and has a background in traditional Kerala cuisine.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
--- സെഷൻ ---
രോഗി [[PATIENT_NAME|Mary Thomas]] [[AGE|56]] [[GENDER|Female]]
അപ്പോയിന്റ്‌മെന്റ് [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0815-001]] പോർട്ടൽ [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
ക്ലയന്റ് ഐപി [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] ഡിവൈസ് IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] ഫോൺ [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
ഇമെയിൽ [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|mary.thomas@example.com]] ആശുപത്രി [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Medical College Hospital Thrissur]]
--- ചാറ്റ് ---
രോഗി: നമസ്കാരം ഡോക്ടർ, കഴിഞ്ഞ മൂന്ന് ദിവസമായി എനിക്ക് തുടർച്ചയായ തലവേദനയുണ്ട്. വേദന പ്രധാനമായും നെറ്റിയിലാണ്, മുന്നോട്ട് കുനിയുമ്പോൾ അത് കൂടുന്നു. എനിക്ക് ഓക്കാനവും വെളിച്ചത്തോടുള്ള സെൻസിറ്റിവിറ്റിയും അനുഭവപ്പെടുന്നു.

ഡോ. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Menon]]: ഹലോ Dr. Anjali Menon, നിങ്ങളുടെ ആശങ്ക എനിക്ക് മനസ്സിലാകുന്നു. ഈ തലവേദനകളെക്കുറിച്ച് കൂടുതൽ പറയാമോ? അവ കൃത്യമായി എപ്പോൾ തുടങ്ങി, അവ മെച്ചപ്പെടാനോ വഷളാകാനോ എന്താണ് കാരണം?

രോഗി: അവ തിങ്കളാഴ്ച രാവിലെ തുടങ്ങി. വേദന സ്പന്ദിക്കുന്നതുപോലെയാണ്, ഞാൻ പാരസെറ്റമോൾ കഴിക്കുന്നുണ്ട്, അത് താൽക്കാലിക ആശ്വാസം നൽകുന്നു. എനിക്ക് നേരിയ പനിയും ശരീരവേദനയും ഉണ്ട്. ഇത് ഗൗരവകരമായ ഒന്നായിരിക്കുമെന്ന് ഞാൻ ആശങ്കപ്പെടുന്നു.

ഡോ. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Menon]]: ശരി. പനിയും ഫോട്ടോഫോബിയയുമുള്ള തലവേദനയുടെ ലക്ഷണങ്ങൾ കണക്കിലെടുത്ത്, നമ്മൾ ജാഗ്രത പാലിക്കേണ്ടതുണ്ട്. നിങ്ങൾക്ക് അടുത്തകാലത്ത് തലയ്ക്ക് എന്തെങ്കിലും പരിക്കേൽക്കുകയുണ്ടായോ അല്ലെങ്കിൽ ഹൈപ്പർടെൻഷൻ അല്ലെങ്കിൽ പ്രമേഹം പോലുള്ള അറിയപ്പെടുന്ന എന്തെങ്കിലും ആരോഗ്യപ്രശ്നങ്ങൾ ഉണ്ടോ?

രോഗി: അല്ല ഡോക്ടർ, എനിക്ക് വിട്ടുമാറാത്ത അസുഖങ്ങളൊന്നുമില്ല. എന്റെ രക്തസമ്മർദ്ദം സാധാരണ നിലയിലാണ്. ഞാൻ തൃശ്ശൂർ നഗരത്തിൽ ഒറ്റയ്ക്കാണ് താമസിക്കുന്നത്, എന്റെ ഭർത്താവ് കൊച്ചിയിലാണ് ജോലി ചെയ്യുന്നത്. ഞാൻ ഒരു ഗൃഹനാഥയാണ്, പഠിപ്പിക്കലിൽ നിന്ന് വിരമിച്ചു.

ഡോ. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Menon]]: ആ വിവരങ്ങൾ പങ്കുവെച്ചതിന് നന്ദി. നിങ്ങളുടെ ലക്ഷണങ്ങൾ കണക്കിലെടുത്ത്, മെനിഞ്ചൈറ്റിസ് അല്ലെങ്കിൽ എൻസെഫലൈറ്റിസ് എന്നിവയെക്കുറിച്ച് എനിക്ക് ആശങ്കയുണ്ട്. ഞാൻ ചില രക്തപരിശോധനകളും തലയുടെ സിടി സ്കാനും ക്രമീകരിക്കാൻ ആഗ്രഹിക്കുന്നു. ദയവായി ഉടൻ ആശുപത്രിയിൽ പോയി ഈ പരിശോധനകൾ ചെയ്യുക.

രോഗി: ശരി ഡോക്ടർ, ഞാൻ ഉടൻ തന്നെ മെഡിക്കൽ കോളേജ് ഹോസ്പിറ്റൽ തൃശ്ശൂരിലേക്ക് പോകാം. എനിക്ക് പാരസെറ്റമോൾ തുടരണമോ? എന്റെ പനിയെക്കുറിച്ച് എന്ത് ചെയ്യണം?

ഡോ. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Menon]]: അതെ, പനിക്കും വേദനയ്ക്കും പാരസെറ്റമോൾ തുടരുക. ധാരാളം ദ്രാവകങ്ങൾ കുടിക്കുക. ആശുപത്രിയിൽ എത്തുമ്പോൾ, നിങ്ങളുടെ ടെലിമെഡിസിൻ കൺസൾട്ടേഷനെക്കുറിച്ച് അവരെ അറിയിക്കുക. പരിശോധനാ ഫലങ്ങൾ കൃത്യമായ കാരണം കണ്ടെത്താനും അനുയോജ്യമായ ചികിത്സ ആരംഭിക്കാനും ഞങ്ങളെ സഹായിക്കും.

രോഗി: നന്ദി ഡോക്ടർ. ഞാൻ നിങ്ങളെ വിവരങ്ങൾ അറിയിക്കും. എനിക്ക് പരിശോധനകൾക്ക് ശേഷം നിങ്ങളെ വിളിക്കാമോ?

ഡോ. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Menon]]: അതെ, റിപ്പോർട്ടുകൾ ലഭിച്ചതിന് ശേഷം ദയവായി എന്നെ വിളിക്കുക. കഴുത്ത് വലിഞ്ഞു മുറുകുക, ആശയക്കുഴപ്പം, അല്ലെങ്കിൽ കഠിനമായ ഛർദ്ദി തുടങ്ങിയ ഗുരുതരമായ ലക്ഷണങ്ങൾ കണ്ടാൽ, ദയവായി ഉടൻ എമർജൻസി വിഭാഗത്തിൽ പോകുക. ശ്രദ്ധിക്കൂ Dr. Anjali Menon.
payload.generated_text_en:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Mary Thomas]] [[AGE|56]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0815-001]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|mary.thomas@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Medical College Hospital Thrissur]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Namaskaram Doctor, I'm having persistent headaches for the past three days. The pain is mainly in the frontal region and gets worse when I bend forward. I also feel some nausea and sensitivity to light.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Menon]]: Hello Mary, I understand your concern. Can you tell me more about these headaches? When did they start exactly, and what makes them better or worse?

Patient: They started on Monday morning. The pain is throbbing and I've been taking paracetamol which gives temporary relief. I also have some mild fever and body ache. I'm worried it might be something serious.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Menon]]: I see. Given your symptoms of headache with fever and photophobia, we need to be cautious. Have you experienced any recent head injury or do you have any known medical conditions like hypertension or diabetes?

Patient: No doctor, I don't have any chronic illnesses. My blood pressure has been normal. I live alone in Thrissur city and my husband works in Kochi. I'm a homemaker and retired from teaching.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Menon]]: Thank you for sharing that information. Based on your symptoms, I'm concerned about possible meningitis or encephalitis. I would like to arrange for some blood tests and a CT scan of your head. Please go to the hospital immediately and get these investigations done.

Patient: Okay doctor, I'll go to Medical College Hospital Thrissur right away. Should I continue with the paracetamol? What about my fever?

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Menon]]: Yes, continue paracetamol for fever and pain. Also take plenty of fluids. When you reach the hospital, inform them about your telemedicine consultation. The test results will help us determine the exact cause and start appropriate treatment.

Patient: Thank you doctor. I'll keep you updated. Should I call you after the tests?

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Menon]]: Yes, please call me after you get the reports. If you develop any severe symptoms like stiff neck, confusion, or severe vomiting, please go to the emergency department immediately. Take care Mary.

payload.professional_persona: Megha John, a seasoned construction and agricultural labourer, blends hands-on masonry, basic carpentry and irrigation expertise with meticulous planning and a collaborative spirit, and they aim to become a foreman who values reliability and community-focused teamwork.
payload.linguistic_persona: Megha John is fluent in Malayalam, reads and writes it proficiently, handles official and everyday English with functional competence, and can follow simple Hindi phrases when needed.
payload.sports_persona: Megha John enjoys playing badminton as a member of the local Kovalam Badminton Club, follows Kerala Blasters FC with enthusiasm, and stays fit through daily morning walks along the backwaters and occasional yoga sessions.
payload.arts_persona: Megha John delights in singing church hymns and Malayalam folk songs, joining Thiruvathirakali dance circles at festivals, and watching classic Malayalam films starring Mohanlal and Manju Warrier, while also enjoying the poetry of O N V Kurup and the soulful melodies of K. S. Chithra.
payload.travel_persona: Megha John prefers modest, well-planned trips that blend nature and culture: she has spent weekends exploring the tea hills of Munnar, the sandy shores of Varkala, and the historic ruins of Hampi, and she dreams of trekking the Swiss Alps while balancing family responsibilities.
payload.culinary_persona: Megha John loves cooking traditional Kerala fare such as appam with stew, spicy fish curry made with tamarind, coconut oil and fresh curry leaves, and banana leaf rice, delighting in the aroma of roasted coconut, while she often enjoys a simple snack of roasted plantain and a cup of strong filter coffee.
payload.persona: Megha John is a practical, community-driven go-getter who blends her love for coconut-framed village life with a relentless pursuit of skill-building, even if she sometimes procrastates on paperwork and forgets to water her plants.
payload.cultural_background: Megha John is a Malayali Christian from a rural part of Thiruvananthapuram in Kerala. She grew up amidst the coconut groves and backwaters, celebrating Onam with her family while also observing Christmas and Easter with her church community. Her cultural identity blends the Kerala coastal lifestyle, the communal values of village life, and the traditions of the Saint Thomas Christian community, such as attending weekly mass, participating in church feasts, and enjoying Malayalam folk songs and dance.
payload.linguistic_background: Megha's mother tongue is Malayalam, and she is fully literate in reading and writing Malayalam. She completed a graduate degree in arts, which required proficiency in English, so she can understand, read and write English at a functional level for official documents and basic communication. She does not have a second or third language, though she can follow simple Hindi phrases learned in school.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Megha has practical skills acquired through years of labour work in construction and agriculture, including masonry, carpentry basics, irrigation maintenance, and planting. She is adept at organising community tasks, coordinating groups for festivals and local development projects, and maintains basic bookkeeping for small co‑ops. Her graduate education equips her with good reading, writing, and communication abilities in English and Malayalam, allowing her to manage paperwork and liaise with supervisors. Additionally, she is proficient in using simple digital tools like mobile phones for messaging and basic internet browsing.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Construction (masonry)", "Basic carpentry", "Agricultural labor", "Irrigation maintenance", "Community task coordination", "Basic bookkeeping", "English-Malayalam documentation", "Digital communication (mobile)"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Megha enjoys participating in group activities such as local folk dance (Thiruvathirakali) and church choir singing. She loves gardening, especially tending to coconut and banana trees in her backyard. In her free time she reads Malayalam novels and poetry, watches Malayalam movies, and volunteers at her church’s community kitchen during festivals. She also likes cooking traditional Kerala dishes like appam and fish curry.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Malayalam (code=ml, script=Malayalam). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
MATERNAL SAFETY: clinical content must be age-plausible for persona age=32. Routine ANC/delivery typically ages 18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC/PNC/contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy as a routine story.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Kerala, district=Thiruvananthapuram.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Malayalam for Malayalam.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 32
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Labourer, Other
- education_level: Graduate & above
- zone: Rural
- state: Kerala
- district: Thiruvananthapuram
- first_language: Malayalam
- clinical_persona_summary: Megha John is a 32-year-old female labourer from a rural area in Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala, who is currently married and holds a graduate degree. Her first language is Malayalam, and she works in construction and agriculture, aiming to become a foreman. She has a practical, community-oriented lifestyle with a focus on skill-building.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
--- സെഷൻ ---
രോഗി [[PATIENT_NAME|Megha John]] [[AGE|32]] [[GENDER|Female]]
അപ്പോയിന്റ്‌മെന്റ് [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0815-001]] പോർട്ടൽ [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
ക്ലയന്റ് ഐപി [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] ഡിവൈസ് IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] ഫോൺ [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
ഇമെയിൽ [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|megha.john@example.com]] ആശുപത്രി [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government Medical College Thiruvananthapuram]]
--- ചാറ്റ് ---
രോഗി: നമസ്കാരം ഡോക്ടർ, ഞാൻ മേഘ ജോൺ, 32 വയസ്സുള്ള, 5 മാസം ഗർഭിണി. എനിക്ക് കഠിനമായ നടുവേദനയും കാലുകളിൽ വീക്കവും അനുഭവപ്പെടുന്നു. ഇത് സാധാരണമാണോ?
ഡോ. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Menon]]: നമസ്കാരം മേഘു, നിങ്ങളുടെ ആശങ്ക എനിക്ക് മനസ്സിലാകുന്നു. ഗർഭകാലത്തെ നടുവേദനയും ചെറിയ തോതിലുള്ള വീക്കവും സാധാരണമാണ്, പക്ഷേ നമ്മൾ അത് സൂക്ഷ്മമായി നിരീക്ഷിക്കണം. വേദനയെക്കുറിച്ച് കൂടുതൽ പറയാമോ - അത് മൂർച്ചയുള്ളതാണോ അതോ മങ്ങിയതാണോ?
രോഗി: അത് മങ്ങിയ വേദനയാണ്, പ്രത്യേകിച്ച് ജോലിസ്ഥലത്ത് ദീർഘനേരം നിൽക്കുമ്പോൾ. വീക്കം ആദ്യം കണങ്കാലുകളിൽ തുടങ്ങി ഇപ്പോൾ മുട്ടറ്റം വരെ എത്തിയിട്ടുണ്ട്. ഞാൻ നിർമ്മാണ മേഖലയിൽ തൊഴിലാളിയായി ജോലി ചെയ്യുന്നതുകൊണ്ട് എനിക്ക് ആശങ്കയുണ്ട്.
ഡോ. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Menon]]: നിങ്ങളുടെ ജോലി സാഹചര്യം എനിക്ക് മനസ്സിലാകുന്നു. നടുവേദനയ്ക്ക്, ദീർഘനേരം നിൽക്കുന്നത് ഒഴിവാക്കാൻ ശ്രമിക്കുക. സാധ്യമാകുമ്പോൾ, പാദങ്ങൾ ഉയർത്തി വെച്ച് ഇരിക്കുക. വീക്കത്തിന്, നിങ്ങൾക്ക് കഴിയുന്നത്ര തവണ കാലുകൾ ഉയർത്തി വെക്കുക. നിർദ്ദേശിച്ച പ്രകാരം നിങ്ങൾ ഇരുമ്പും കാൽസ്യവും അടങ്ങിയ ഗുളികകൾ കഴിക്കുന്നുണ്ടോ?
രോഗി: അതെ, ഞാൻ ദിവസവും കഴിക്കുന്നുണ്ട്. എന്റെ അവസാനത്തെ ANC പരിശോധന 2 ആഴ്ച മുമ്പ് പ്രൈമറി ഹെൽത്ത് സെന്ററിൽ ആയിരുന്നു. എന്റെ ഹീമോഗ്ലോബിൻ 9.2 ആയിരുന്നു. വീക്കം കുഞ്ഞിനെ ബാധിക്കുമോ എന്നോർത്ത് ഞാൻ ആശങ്കപ്പെടേണ്ടതുണ്ടോ?
ഡോ. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Menon]]: നിങ്ങളുടെ ഹീമോഗ്ലോബിൻ അല്പം കുറവാണ്, പക്ഷേ അത് നിയന്ത്രിക്കാവുന്നതാണ്. വീക്കം കൊണ്ട് മാത്രം കുഞ്ഞിനെ ബാധിക്കില്ല, പക്ഷേ പ്രീ-എക്ലാംപ്സിയയുടെ ലക്ഷണങ്ങൾ - കഠിനമായ തലവേദന, കാഴ്ചയിലെ മാറ്റങ്ങൾ, അല്ലെങ്കിൽ പെട്ടെന്നുള്ള ഭാരക്കൂടുതൽ എന്നിവ നമ്മൾ നിരീക്ഷിക്കണം. ഇവയിൽ ഏതെങ്കിലും നിങ്ങൾ അനുഭവിച്ചിട്ടുണ്ടോ?
രോഗി: തലവേദനയോ കാഴ്ചപ്രശ്നങ്ങളോ ഇല്ല. എന്റെ ഭാരം സ്ഥിരമാണ്. നടുവേദനയ്ക്ക് പ്രത്യേകിച്ച് ഞാൻ എന്ത് ചെയ്യണം?

രോഗി: ഞാൻ എന്റെ സൂപ്പർവൈസറോട് സംസാരിക്കാൻ ശ്രമിക്കാം. അടുത്ത പരിശോധനയ്ക്ക് ഞാൻ എപ്പോൾ വരണം?
ഡോ. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Menon]]: നിങ്ങളുടെ അടുത്ത ANC പരിശോധന അടുത്ത ആഴ്ചത്തേക്ക് നിശ്ചയിച്ചിട്ടുണ്ട്. വേദന കൂടുകയോ വീക്കം വേഗത്തിൽ വർദ്ധിക്കുകയോ ചെയ്താൽ ഉടൻ ഞങ്ങളെ ബന്ധപ്പെടുക. കൂടാതെ, വീട്ടിൽ രക്തസമ്മർദ്ദം അളക്കാൻ ഒരു ഉപകരണം ഉണ്ടെങ്കിൽ അത് നിരീക്ഷിക്കുക.
രോഗി: നന്ദി ഡോക്ടർ. ഒരു ചോദ്യം കൂടി - പ്രസവം വരെ ജോലി ചെയ്യുന്നത് സുരക്ഷിതമാണോ?
