संदर्भ पत्र [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815]] कडून [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Rural Health Centre Hingoli]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Prakash Kadam]]
विषय: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Patil]], [[AGE|67]] / [[GENDER|Male]], जिल्हा [[DISTRICT|Hingoli]]
कारण: गेल्या तीन आठवड्यांपासून सतत खोकला आणि श्वास घेण्यास त्रास, सोबत टाइप 2 मधुमेह आणि उच्च रक्तदाबाचा इतिहास आहे.

आदरणीय महोदय/महोदया,

आम्ही आमच्या रुग्णाला पुढील मूल्यमापन आणि व्यवस्थापनासाठी संदर्भित करत आहोत. रुग्ण रमेश पाटील, हिंघोळीतील ग्रामीण भागातील 67 वर्षांचा पुरुष ऑपरेटर. रुग्णाची मुख्य तक्रार उत्पादक खोकला आणि वाढता श्वास घेण्यास त्रास आहे, जो गेल्या तीन आठवड्यांपासून वाढत आहे. रुग्ण सांगतो की त्याला सोबत सौम्य ताप आणि कधीकधी छातीत अस्वस्थता जाणवते.

तपासणीत, रुग्ण शुद्धीवर आणि जागरूक आहे. त्याचे महत्त्वाचे संकेत दाखवतात की रक्तदाब 140/90 mmHg, हृदय गती 98 bpm, श्वसन दर मिनिटाला 22 श्वास आणि तापमान 100.4°F आहे. सामान्य हवेवर ऑक्सिजन सॅच्युरेशन 91% आहे. छातीची तपासणी केल्यावर दोन्ही बाजूंना घरघर आवाज ऐकू येतो, जो खालच्या भागात जास्त स्पष्ट आहे.

रुग्णाला टाइप 2 मधुमेह आणि उच्च रक्तदाबाचा जुना इतिहास आहे, ज्यासाठी तो तोंडावाटे औषधे घेत आहे. अलीकडे त्याचे रक्तातील साखर पातळी अस्थिर आहे. सुरुवातीच्या छातीच्या एक्स-रे मध्ये न्यूमोनिया सुचवणारे इन्फिल्ट्रेट्स दिसतात. त्याचे वय, इतर आजार आणि श्वासोच्छवासाच्या लक्षणांची तीव्रता लक्षात घेता, त्याला उच्च स्तराच्या उपचारांची गरज आहे असे आम्हाला वाटते.

आम्ही तुम्हाला विनंती करतो की कृपया रुग्णाचे मूल्यमापन करावे, निदान निश्चित करावे आणि योग्य उपचार सुरू करावेत. आम्ही त्याचे वैद्यकीय रेकॉर्ड्स, ज्यामध्ये अलीकडील लॅब रिपोर्ट्स आणि इमेजिंग समाविष्ट आहे, तुमच्या तपासणीसाठी पाठवले आहेत.

कोणत्याही पुढील माहितीसाठी, तुम्ही आमच्याशी [[PHONE_NUMBER|9423012345]] वर संपर्क साधू शकता.

धन्यवाद.

आपला विश्वासू,
डॉ. प्रकाश कदम
[[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Prakash Kadam]]
[[HOSPITAL_NAME|Rural Health Centre Hingoli]]
[[MRN|MRN-RHCH-2024-0678]]
[[RELATIVE_NAME|Savitri Patil]]
[[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P1234567]]
[[DRIVING_LICENCE|MH04 2010001234]]
payload.generated_text_en:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Rural Health Centre Hingoli]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Prakash Kadam]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Patil]], [[AGE|67]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|Hingoli]]
Reason: Persistent cough and breathlessness for the past three weeks, with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension.

Dear Sir/Madam,

This is to refer our patient, Ramesh Patil, a 67-year-old male operator from a rural area in Hingoli, for further evaluation and management. The patient presents with a chief complaint of a productive cough and progressive shortness of breath, which has been worsening over the last three weeks. He reports associated low-grade fever and occasional chest discomfort.

On examination, the patient is conscious and oriented. His vitals show a blood pressure of 140/90 mmHg, heart rate of 98 bpm, respiratory rate of 22 breaths per minute, and a temperature of 100.4°F. Oxygen saturation on room air is 91%. Auscultation of the chest reveals bilateral crackles, more prominent in the lower zones.

The patient has a known history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension, for which he is on oral medications. His blood sugar levels have been erratic recently. An initial chest X-ray shows infiltrates suggestive of pneumonia. Given his age, comorbidities, and the severity of his respiratory symptoms, we feel he requires a higher level of care.

We request you to kindly evaluate the patient, confirm the diagnosis, and initiate appropriate treatment. We have also sent his medical records, including recent lab reports and imaging, for your review.

For any further information, you may contact us at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9423012345]].

Thank you.

Sincerely,
Dr. Prakash Kadam
[[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Prakash Kadam]]
[[HOSPITAL_NAME|Rural Health Centre Hingoli]]
[[MRN|MRN-RHCH-2024-0678]]
[[RELATIVE_NAME|Savitri Patil]]
[[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P1234567]]
[[DRIVING_LICENCE|MH04 2010001234]]

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.

  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Nepali (code=ne, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=West Bengal, district=Darjiling.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Nepali.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 18
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Labourer, Agriculture
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: West Bengal
- district: Darjiling
- first_language: Nepali
- clinical_persona_summary: An 18-year-old female labourer in agriculture from rural West Bengal, never married, with primary education. She speaks Nepali as her first language and has limited Bengali proficiency. Her lifestyle includes physical work in tea gardens and occasional consumption of local foods.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.professional_persona: Kaushalyabai Jagannath, a seasoned community organiser and domestic specialist, leverages flawless budgeting, traditional cooking, event coordination and needlework to mentor younger women and sustain cultural rituals, while maintaining a disciplined routine and exacting standards.
payload.linguistic_persona: Kaushalyabai Jagannath, fluent in spoken Marathi and Khandeshi and conversational in Hindi, relies on vivid oral storytelling and mental recall to manage household tasks, community events and resolve disputes, despite being unable to read or write.
payload.sports_persona: Kaushalyabai Jagannath, who prefers gentle exercise, joins early morning walks in the local market area and attends weekly yoga sessions at the community centre, while cheering for the Mumbai Indians cricket team and occasionally playing traditional board games like Ludo with neighbours.
payload.arts_persona: Kaushalyabai Jagannath, a devoted listener of Marathi Lavani, devotional bhajans and classic Hindi films, often hums melodies of Lata Mangeshkar and Sudhir Phadke while knitting, and appreciates the theatrical performances of local Natak mandalis.
payload.travel_persona: Kaushalyabai Jagannath, who enjoys short pilgrimages to Shirdi and Pandharpur and occasional weekend getaways to Mumbai, plans trips meticulously using word of mouth and prefers traveling by train or shared auto, balancing family commitments and limited budget.
payload.culinary_persona: Kaushalyabai Jagannath, a seasoned home cook, excels at preparing poha, bhakri, puran poli and shrikhand, prefers using jaggery, kokum, fresh neem leaves and turmeric in her dishes, and enjoys savoring traditional sweets spiced with cardamom and garnished with marigold petals during festivals.
payload.persona: Kaushalyabai Jagannath, a meticulous 64‑year‑old matriarch who blends disciplined budgeting, traditional cooking and community leadership, yet battles chronic worries and occasional splurges on festival indulgences.
payload.cultural_background: Kaushalyabai belongs to a traditional Marathi‑speaking family settled in Jalna, Maharashtra, a region where Marathi culture intermingles with Khandeshi folk traditions. She was raised in a joint family that values hierarchical respect, community celebrations, and the observance of regional festivals such as Ganesh Chaturthi, Diwali, and the Khandeshi Jatra. Even though Jalna is an urban setting, her upbringing is steeped in the conventional Maharashtrian ethos of hospitality, disciplined routine, and reverence for elders.
payload.linguistic_background: As an illiterate woman, Kaushalyabai cannot read or write in any script, but she is fluent in spoken Marathi, her mother tongue, and Khandeshi, the regional dialect she uses with neighbours and relatives. She also understands basic Hindi from television and community interactions, though she relies on oral communication. Her linguistic skills are practical and centred on daily household interactions, religious rituals, and the organization of community events, where she excels in giving clear verbal instructions and remembering intricate details through mental notes.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Kaushalyabai has mastered a suite of domestic and community skills through years of hands‑on experience. She excels in preparing authentic Maharashtrian dishes such as poha, bhakri, and shrikhand, maintaining strict hygienic standards in the kitchen. She manages the household budget meticulously, using mental arithmetic and simple ledger marks despite being illiterate. Her organizational talent shines in planning family events and temple festivals, where she coordinates volunteers, prepares timelines, and ensures every ritual is performed on schedule. Additionally, she is skilled in traditional needlework and embroidery, crafts herbal remedies for common ailments, and acts as a trusted mediator in neighbourhood disputes, leveraging her assertive yet compassionate demeanor.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Maharashtrian Cooking", "Household Budget Management", "Event Planning for Family and Temple", "Traditional Needlework and Embroidery", "Herbal Remedy Preparation", "Community Mediation", "Mental Arithmetic and Record-Keeping", "Gardening and Household Organization"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Kaushalyabai enjoys gardening, especially tending to marigold and neem plants, knitting simple woolen shawls, listening to Marathi Lavani and devotional bhajans, preparing traditional sweets like puran poli during festivals, taking leisurely walks in the local market, attending small group discussions at the community centre, and watching classic Hindi cinema on television. She also finds pleasure in keeping a meticulous inventory of pantry staples and organizing family photo albums.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Gardening (marigold & neem)", "Knitting woolen shawls", "Listening to Marathi Lavani and devotional bhajans", "Preparing traditional sweets (puran poli) during festivals", "Leisurely walks in the local market", "Participating in small group discussions at community centre", "Watching classic Hindi cinema on TV", "Maintaining pantry inventory and photo album organization"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Although retired, Kaushalyabai aims to continue serving her family and community by mentoring younger women in preserving traditional cooking, budgeting, and cultural rituals. She aspires to document her family's recipes and oral histories through audio recordings, ensuring they are passed down to future generations. She also seeks to strengthen the local women's self‑help group by organizing skill‑sharing workshops, thereby fostering financial independence and social support for the neighbourhood, while maintaining her own mental well‑being through a disciplined daily routine.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 64-year-old female, currently married, retired homemaker with illiterate education, residing in an urban area of Maharashtra, Jalna district. She is a Marathi-speaking woman with limited literacy but fluent in spoken Marathi and regional dialect. Clinically, she exhibits traditional lifestyle patterns with potential dietary habits centered on local staples and possible stress-related behaviors.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.

- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ER Triage & Nursing Notes for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Marathi (code=mr, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Jalna.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Marathi.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once in the arrival/RTA/ambulance line. Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 64
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Jalna
- first_language: Marathi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 64-year-old female, currently married, retired homemaker with illiterate education, residing in an urban area of Maharashtra, Jalna district. She is a Marathi-speaking woman with limited literacy but fluent in spoken Marathi and regional dialect. Clinically, she exhibits traditional lifestyle patterns with potential dietary habits centered on local staples and possible stress-related behaviors.

Document format guide for ER Triage & Nursing Notes (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|...]]
Arrived by ambulance / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|...]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Vitals / acuity: <short notes>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Savitri Bai]] [[AGE|64]] [[GENDER|Female]] वॉर्ड [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] बेड [[BED_NUMBER|15]]
रुग्णवाहिकेने सकाळी 08:30 वाजता आले. वाहनाचा नोंदणी क्रमांक: [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|MH-22-AB-1234]].

आजाराचा इतिहास: रुग्ण 64 वर्षांची महिला आहे जी आज सकाळी घरी बाथरूममध्ये घसरली. तिला डाव्या कंबरेत तात्काळ तीव्र वेदना झाल्याचे सांगते आणि ती वजन पेलण्यास असमर्थ होती. शुद्ध हरपल्याची कोणतीही नोंद नाही. रुग्ण इतर कोणत्याही दुखापतींचा नकार देते.
जीवनचिन्हे / तातडी: GCS 15, BP 140/90 mmHg, HR 95 bpm, RR 18/min, SpO2 98% सामान्य हवेत, तापमान 98.4°F. डाव्या कंबरेत वेदना गुण 8/10.
परिचर्या मूल्यांकन: रुग्ण शुद्धीत आणि दिशाभूल आहे. डावा खालचा पाय आखूड आणि बाहेरच्या बाजूला वळलेला आहे. डाव्या ग्रेटर ट्रोकॅन्टरवर स्पर्शाने लक्षणीय वेदना जाणवते. उघड्या जखमा आढळल्या नाहीत. त्वचा अखंड आहे.
हस्तक्षेप: रुग्णाला एनपीओ स्थितीत ठेवले. उजव्या हातावर 18G कॅन्युलासह आयव्ही ॲक्सेस स्थापित केला. 100 mL/hr वेगाने 0.9% नॉर्मल सलाईन इन्फ्युजन सुरू केले. वेदनाशामक दिले: नियमानुसार Morphine 5mg IV. रुग्णाला दीर्घ श्वसन व्यायाम आणि इन्सेंटिव्ह स्पायरोमेट्रीबद्दल शिकवले. रुग्ण आणि पतीला कंबरेच्या खबरदारीबद्दल समजावून सांगितले. अस्थिरोग सल्ला मागवला.
योजना: डाव्या कंबरेचे तातडीचे क्ष-किरण (एपी आणि लॅटरल) घेण्यात आले. शस्त्रक्रिया स्थिरीकरणासाठी अस्थिरोग टीमकडून मूल्यमापन केले जाईल. वेदना व्यवस्थापन आणि आयव्ही फ्लुइड्स सुरू ठेवा. जीवनचिन्हे दर तासाला तपासा. शस्त्रक्रियेच्या निर्णयाची प्रतीक्षा.
payload.generated_text_en:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Jalna District Civil Hospital]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0912-001]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Savitri Bai]] [[AGE|64]] [[GENDER|Female]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|15]]
Arrived by ambulance at 08:30 hours. Vehicle registration: [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|MH-22-AB-1234]].
Accompanied by husband, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Patil]]. Contact number: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]].
Attending doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vijay Deshmukh]].
Chief Complaint: Fall at home with left hip pain.
History of Present Illness: Patient is a 64-year-old female who reportedly slipped in the bathroom at home this morning. She reports immediate severe pain in the left hip and was unable to bear weight. No loss of consciousness reported. Patient denies any other injuries.
Vitals / Acuity: GCS 15, BP 140/90 mmHg, HR 95 bpm, RR 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 98.4°F. Pain score 8/10 at left hip.
Nursing Assessment: Patient is alert and oriented x3. Left lower extremity is shortened and externally rotated. Significant tenderness to palpation over the left greater trochanter. No open wounds noted. Skin is intact.
Interventions: Patient placed on NPO status. IV access established in right hand with 18G cannula. 0.9% Normal Saline infusion started at 100 mL/hr. Analgesia administered: Morphine 5mg IV as per protocol. Patient educated on deep breathing exercises and incentive spirometry. Hip precautions explained to patient and husband. Orthopedic consult called.
Plan: STAT X-rays of left hip (AP and lateral) obtained. Orthopedic team to evaluate for surgical fixation. Continue pain management and IV fluids. Monitor vitals q1h. Awaiting surgical decision.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

Her lifestyle is characterized by disciplined routines, a love for folk arts, and limited financial resources.

[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.professional_persona: Sorona Bag, an 18-year-old tea-garden labourer in Darjeeling, blends practical expertise in leaf plucking, pruning and organic soil care with a curiosity for new horticultural methods, while they maintain reliable teamwork and keen attention to safety, positioning them for a future supervisory role.
payload.linguistic_persona: Sorona Bag, a native Nepali speaker who reads and writes the language fluently, uses basic English only to follow work instructions, understands a handful of Bengali words but cannot read its script, recognises simple prayers in Devanagari and Arabic scripts, and they prefer solitary tasks while comfortably navigating multilingual interactions when needed.
payload.sports_persona: Sorona Bag, who enjoys playing football on the tea-garden’s open field with a small group of friends and occasionally joins a local women’s badminton club, values the physical stamina built from garden work, prefers modest team sizes, and they stay active despite a tendency to avoid large crowds.
payload.arts_persona: Sorona Bag, a devoted listener of Nepali folk music such as the soulful renditions of Anju Panta and the classic duets of Udit Narayan, often sings along while weaving bamboo baskets, cherishes the traditional dance steps taught during Lhosar celebrations, and they find artistic expression a soothing escape from the rigors of garden labor.
payload.travel_persona: Sorona Bag, who prefers modest, well-planned trips by train or bus, has explored nearby Kalimpong and the tea-garden town of Darjeeling, dreams of trekking in Sikkim’s alpine valleys, enjoys staying in family-run guesthouses, and they carefully budget each journey to avoid financial strain.
payload.culinary_persona: Sorona Bag, who enjoys cooking simple Nepali staples like dal-bhat with simmered vegetables, spicy chicken momos, and tangy aloo achar using home-grown chilies and coriander, prefers the comfort of familiar flavors over fancy cuisines, and they often share meals with neighbours during communal feasts.
payload.persona: Sorona Bag, an 18-year-old tea-garden workaholic who balances a quiet love for Nepali folk tunes and bamboo weaving with a restless worry about their family’s future, they often juggle frugal budgeting with occasional sweet indulgences.
payload.cultural_background: Sorona belongs to the Nepali‑speaking Gorkha community of the Darjeeling tea garden region, which blends Nepali cultural traditions—such as folk songs, dance, and the celebration of Lhosar—with a distinct Muslim identity that shapes her religious practices and social customs. Growing up in a rural setting of West Bengal, she is also familiar with the agrarian way of life, tea‑plantation labour, and the inter‑communal harmony typical of the hill districts.
payload.linguistic_background: Sorona's mother tongue is Nepali, which she reads and writes fluently at a primary‑school level. She can converse comfortably in Nepali and understands everyday speech. Because she lives in West Bengal, she has picked up basic Bengali words but cannot read the script. She has limited exposure to Hindi and Urdu, mainly through religious prayers, and can recognise simple prayers in the Devanagari/Arabic script. Her formal schooling stopped at primary, so her English is rudimentary, enough for basic instructions at work.
payload.skills_and_expertise: As a labourer in the Darjeeling tea gardens, Sorona has practical expertise in tea leaf plucking, pruning, and maintaining garden rows, as well as knowledge of soil preparation and organic fertiliser application. She is proficient in basic horticulture tasks such as planting tuber crops (e.g., potatoes) and caring for household vegetable gardens. Additionally, she possesses domestic skills—cooking traditional Nepali dishes, stitching simple garments, and managing household budgeting. Her routine work demands physical endurance, teamwork, and attention to safety protocols.

payload.hobbies_and_interests: Sorona enjoys listening to Nepali folk songs and occasionally singing along while working in the garden. She likes to read simple storybooks in Nepali, especially those about nature and village life. In her spare time, she tends to a small kitchen garden where she grows chilies, coriander, and leafy greens. She also likes weaving small baskets from bamboo strips, a traditional craft taught by older women in her community. On festivals, she participates in communal feasts and modest celebrations with family and neighbours.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Nepali folk songs', 'Singing while gardening', 'Reading simple Nepali storybooks', 'Maintaining a kitchen garden (chilies, coriander, leafy greens)', 'Weaving bamboo baskets', 'Participating in communal feasts during festivals']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Sorona aspires to move beyond seasonal labour and secure a permanent role as a skilled tea‑garden supervisor, where she can oversee quality control and train younger workers. She hopes to acquire formal training in horticulture or agricultural engineering through a government skill‑development program, aiming to increase her earnings and support her family. In the longer term, she dreams of establishing a small organic vegetable farm on a plot of land she can later inherit, providing sustainable income and contributing to her community’s food security.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=West Bengal, district=Darjiling.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]

payload.travel_persona: Devi W S, they prefer short trips to nearby cultural sites such as Kangla Fort and Loktak Lake, occasional pilgrimages to Puri’s Jagannath Temple, and leisurely visits to Shillong’s tea gardens, planning each journey meticulously while balancing health constraints.
payload.culinary_persona: Devi W S, they excel at cooking Eromba, Singju, and Champhaa, favor fresh coriander, chilies, and seasonal vegetables, and relish sweet rasgulla on festival days.
payload.persona: Devi W S is a curious, organized 79‑year‑old keeper of tradition; despite occasional anxiety and forgetfulness, they lovingly preserve folk stories, recipes, and rituals for their family.
payload.cultural_background: Devi grew up in the urban neighborhoods of Imphal East, Manipur, a culturally vibrant city known for its classical dance forms, handloom traditions, and festivals like Yaoshang and Lai Haraoba. As a Meitei Hindu, her family blended the rich Manipuri heritage with Vaishnavite devotional practices, fostering a deep appreciation for art, ritual, and community cohesion.
payload.linguistic_background: Devi's mother tongue is Manipuri (Meitei), which she uses fluently for daily conversation, storytelling, and cultural rituals. She also communicates in basic English when needed, especially with younger relatives, but because she never attended school, she remains illiterate in both Manipuri and English scripts. She cannot read or write, relying on oral tradition and memory for information.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Devi excels at traditional Manipuri cooking, preparing dishes such as Eromba, Singju, and Champhaa with authentic flavors. She is adept at handloom weaving, creating intricate handwoven shawls and textiles for family use and occasional community gifting. Her lifelong experience as a homemaker endows her with strong household management, including budgeting, planning family events, and caring for elders. She is a gifted oral storyteller, preserving folk tales, myths, and histories of the Meitei people. Additionally, she is knowledgeable in conducting Hindu rituals, preparing puja items, and guiding family members through religious ceremonies.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Traditional Manipuri cooking', 'Handloom weaving', 'Household management and budgeting', 'Oral storytelling of Meitei folklore', 'Conducting Hindu rituals and puja preparations']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Devi enjoys listening to traditional Manipuri folk songs and classical music, especially the pakhawaj and pena performances. She loves spending time in her small garden, tending to marigolds and seasonal vegetables. Reading is not possible, but she likes to sit with her grandchildren as they read storybooks aloud. She also takes pleasure in preparing elaborate puja arrangements during festivals and occasionally knitting simple shawls. Her curiosity leads her to attend community talks about historical art and to experiment with new recipes.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Manipuri folk music', 'Gardening with seasonal vegetables', 'Spending time with grandchildren', 'Preparing puja decorations for festivals', 'Knitting simple shawls', 'Attending community talks on art and culture']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Even at 79, Devi aims to preserve and transmit Manipuri cultural heritage by teaching her grandchildren traditional recipes, weaving techniques, and folk stories. She wishes to support her family's health and education, especially ensuring her great-grandchildren receive good schooling. She also hopes to volunteer at the local temple and community center, helping organize festivals and assisting younger women in mastering household rituals. Her ambition is to leave a legacy of cultural continuity and familial harmony.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 79-year-old female, widowed, retired homemaker from Imphal East, Manipur, with an illiterate education level, who speaks Manipuri as her first language and basic English. She resides in an urban zone and maintains a traditional lifestyle, preserving cultural practices and family rituals. Clinically, she may exhibit occasional anxiety and forgetfulness but remains organized and engaged in community activities. Her routine includes gentle morning walks and participation in senior yoga circles.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.

- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:

GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:

  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →

GOOD:

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Sharma]] | Course / advice: Patient admitted with pulmonary tuberculosis complicated by type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Initial sputum microscopy showed acid-fast bacilli. Chest X-ray revealed cavitary lesions in right upper lobe. Started on standard anti-tubercular therapy (ATT) regimen with isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol. Blood glucose levels monitored and insulin initiated for glycemic control. Blood pressure managed with amlodipine and metformin. Patient responded well to treatment with fever resolution by day 5 and improved appetite. Sputum conversion achieved by week 6. Discharged on continuation phase ATT with regular follow-up. Counselled on medication adherence, nutrition, and lifestyle modifications. Family educated on infection control measures.

payload.linguistic_persona: Heikrujam Lhouvum, fluent in Manipuri (both spoken and the Meitei Mayek script), commands polished English for scholarly discourse, and maintains functional Hindi literacy for everyday interactions, allowing them to translate sacred texts and bridge generational communication gaps.
payload.sports_persona: Heikrujam Lhouvum, an avid football fan, regularly watches NEROCA FC matches, plays weekly badminton at the Imphal East community centre, and joins local marathon runs during the cooler winter months, balancing energetic activity with occasional bouts of anxiety about performance.
payload.arts_persona: Heikrujam Lhouvum, a passionate patron of Manipuri arts, frequently attends Pena concerts by renowned maestro Kh. Mangang Singh, watches contemporary theatre productions at the Sangeet Natak Bhavan, and occasionally performs Ras Lila dances with local youth groups, drawing inspiration from artists like Seema Khaidem and filmmaker Aribam Syam Sharma.
payload.travel_persona: Heikrujam Lhouvum, an exploratory traveler, prefers short, well-planned road trips to nearby hill stations like Shillong and Cherrapunji, craves cultural immersion in historic places such as Varanasi and Bodh Gaya, and dreams of a pilgrimage to the Himalayan monasteries of Ladakh, always budgeting trips carefully to accommodate community responsibilities.
payload.culinary_persona: Heikrujam Lhouvum, a connoisseur of Manipuri cuisine, delights in the fiery tang of Eromba made with boiled vegetables and ngari (fermented fish), savors the fresh crunch of Singju with lotus stem and bamboo shoots, and often treats friends to a steaming bowl of Chak-hao (black rice) pulao seasoned with cumin and mustard leaves, though they rarely cook at home, preferring to explore street stalls.
payload.persona: Heikrujam Lhouvum, a disciplined yet curious cultural priest, balances meticulous community projects with a love for arts, wrestles with frequent worries, and never says no to a good cup of tea.
payload.cultural_background: Heikrujam Lhouvum hails from Imphal East, an urban area of Manipur, and belongs to the Meitei (Manipuri) community. His upbringing was steeped in the rich cultural tapestry of Manipur, where traditional dance forms such as Ras Lila, the classical music of the Pena, and communal festivals like Lai Haraoba are integral parts of daily life. Growing up in a city that blends ancient customs with contemporary influences, he has internalised a strong sense of communal responsibility, respect for elders, and an appreciation for artistic expression that reflects the syncretic heritage of the region.
payload.linguistic_background: He is a native Manipuri speaker, fluent in both spoken Manipuri and the Meitei Mayek script used for reading and writing. His secondary language is English, in which he is highly proficient across speaking, reading, and writing, enabling him to engage with broader academic and religious networks. Formal education through higher secondary level has also provided him with functional literacy in Hindi, though he primarily uses Manipuri and English for daily communication and professional duties.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Heikrujam possesses a blend of religious, cultural, and organizational competencies. As an ordained Sanamahi priest, he is skilled in conducting traditional rites, chanting sacred verses, and interpreting ancient myths. He excels in community outreach, counseling, and leading educational workshops on cultural heritage preservation. His disciplined nature translates into strong project management, event planning, and administrative abilities. Additionally, he has proficiency in the Manipuri language (Meitei Mayek script), English communication, and basic Hindi literacy, enabling effective translation and documentation of religious texts.

payload.hobbies_and_interests: He enjoys attending and occasionally performing traditional Manipuri dance and music sessions, especially the Pena instrument. He is an avid reader of spiritual and philosophical texts, both in Manipuri and English. His curiosity leads him to explore contemporary art galleries and theatre productions in Imphal. He frequently organizes community gatherings, volunteers for local NGOs working on youth empowerment, and practices yoga and meditation to manage stress.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Traditional Manipuri dance', 'Playing the Pena instrument', 'Reading spiritual and philosophical literature', 'Visiting art galleries', 'Attending theatre productions', 'Organizing community gatherings', 'Volunteering with youth NGOs', 'Practicing yoga and meditation']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: He aspires to strengthen the spiritual and cultural fabric of his community by establishing a dedicated Sanamahi cultural center in Imphal East that offers regular workshops on traditional arts, language, and rituals. He aims to train a new generation of lay religious facilitators, expand interfaith dialogue initiatives, and develop youth mentorship programs that integrate ethical teachings with modern education. Over the next decade, he envisions publishing a bilingual compendium of Sanamahi prayers and folklore, and positioning himself as a recognized thought leader in preserving Manipur’s intangible heritage.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
