Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Discharge Summary for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Manipuri (code=mni, script=Meitei). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Manipur, district=Imphal East.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Meitei for Manipuri.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 38
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Ordained Religious Workers, Other
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Manipur
- district: Imphal East
- first_language: Manipuri
- clinical_persona_summary: Male, 38, currently married, ordained religious worker with higher secondary education, living in urban Imphal East, Manipur, and speaking Manipuri. He is prone to anxiety and values community engagement, often seeking comfort in tea. His lifestyle includes regular participation in local cultural events and community projects.

Document format guide for Discharge Summary (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] DOB [[DOB|1994-06-21]] [[AGE|30]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]] | Course / advice: <concise paragraphs matching domain + persona sex/age>

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]

payload.persona: Kalana Behera is a solitary, curiosity‑driven 25‑year‑old who blends traditional cooking and handloom weaving into a modest micro‑enterprise, manages money with meticulousness, and balances disciplined rituals with a love for quiet creativity.
payload.cultural_background: Kalana Behera belongs to a close‑knit Hindu family living in the agrarian village of Jajapur, Odisha. Her upbringing was steeped in the rhythms of rural life – early mornings at the paddy fields, evenings spent listening to folk songs, and festivals like Raja, Durga Puja and Diwali that brighten the mud‑bound lanes. She grew up in a joint household where respect for elders, communal cooking of dishes like pakhala and rasgulla, and participation in temple fairs were everyday practices. While rooted in traditional Odia customs, her curious nature pushes her towards newer ideas in art and craft, making her an avid follower of Sambalpuri handloom designs and Odissi performances.
payload.linguistic_background: Kalana's first language is Odia, which she speaks fluently and can read and write with the basic literacy she acquired before leaving school during the below‑primary stage. She can comfortably follow Odia recipes, read local newspapers, and write simple notes. She also has functional familiarity with Hindi, primarily from religious texts and occasional television programmes; she can understand spoken Hindi but has limited writing ability. Her exposure to English is minimal – she knows a few everyday words from TV and smartphone interfaces and can follow simple instructions, but cannot hold a conversation.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Kalana is proficient in preparing a range of traditional Odia dishes, including dalma, pakhala, and sweets such as rasgulla and chhena poda. She possesses hands‑on experience with Sambalpuri handloom weaving, creating ikat patterns for family use and occasional local sale. Managing household finances, she efficiently plans monthly grocery lists, tracks expenses, and ensures timely bill payments. She is digitally literate at a basic level, comfortable using a smartphone for messaging, browsing recipes, and accessing government welfare schemes. Additionally, she can organize small community gatherings for festivals, coordinate food preparation, and assist in arranging folk performances.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Cooking traditional Odia dishes", "Sambalpuri handloom weaving", "Household budgeting", "Basic digital literacy", "Organizing community gatherings", "Knowledge of folk arts"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Kalana enjoys solitary pursuits that nurture her curiosity and love for the arts. She spends quiet evenings reading Odia literature and mythological tales, and listening to classical Odissi music. She often sketches folk motifs in a small notebook, a hobby that lets her explore creative expression. In her modest backyard she maintains a vegetable patch of amaranth, chillies, and marigolds, finding peace in gardening. She also likes to experiment with new recipes, blending traditional flavors with occasional modern twists, and captures nature’s moments with her phone during early‑morning walks.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Reading Odia literature", "Listening to Odissi classical music", "Sketching folk motifs", "Gardening", "Experimenting with new recipes", "Photography during nature walks"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Kalana aspires to transform her culinary and weaving talents into a modest home‑based micro‑enterprise, producing homemade pickles, chhena poda, and handwoven scarves for sale at the local market. She aims to join a women’s self‑help group to learn basic accounting and obtain a short certificate in home science or community development. In the long term, she wishes to become a resource person in her village, teaching other women traditional cooking and Sambalpuri weaving, thereby preserving cultural heritage while generating a sustainable family income.

You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].

  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →

GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +

ରକ୍ତ ଶର୍କରା (ଉପବାସ): 85 mg/dL (ସାଧାରଣ: 70-100)
ରକ୍ତ ଶର୍କରା (ଭୋଜନ ପରେ): 115 mg/dL (ସାଧାରଣ: <140)
ମୂତ୍ର ପ୍ରୋଟିନ୍: ଅଳ୍ପ (ପ୍ରୋଟିନୁରିଆ ପାଇଁ ନକାରାତ୍ମକ)
ମୂତ୍ର ଶର୍କରା: ନକାରାତ୍ମକ
ମୂତ୍ର ପରୀକ୍ଷା: ସାଧାରଣ
ରକ୍ତ ଗୋଷ୍ଠୀ: O Positive

ଅଲଟ୍ରାସାଉଣ୍ଡ: ଗୋଟିଏ ଜୀବନ୍ତ ଭ୍ରୂଣ, ଆନୁମାନିକ ଭ୍ରୂଣ ଓଜନ 1.1 kg, ଆମ୍ନିଓଟିକ୍ ଫ୍ଲୁଇଡ୍ ଇଣ୍ଡେକ୍ସ ସାଧାରଣ, ପ୍ଲାସେଣ୍ଟା ଆଣ୍ଟେରିଅର୍।

ବ୍ୟାଖ୍ୟା:
ସାମାନ୍ୟ ରକ୍ତହୀନତା ଅଛି, ସମ୍ଭବତଃ ଶାରୀରିକ। ଗର୍ଭାବସ୍ଥା ମଧୁମେହର କୌଣସି ପ୍ରମାଣ ନାହିଁ। ପ୍ରିକ୍ଲାମ୍ପସିଆ ସୂଚାଇବା ପାଇଁ କୌଣସି ପ୍ରୋଟିନୁରିଆ ନାହିଁ। 28 ସପ୍ତାହ ପାଇଁ ଉପଯୁକ୍ତ ଭ୍ରୂଣ ବୃଦ୍ଧି ସହିତ ସଙ୍ଗତିପୂର୍ଣ୍ଣ ଅଲଟ୍ରାସାଉଣ୍ଡ ଅନୁସନ୍ଧାନ। ଲୌହ ପରିପୂରକ ସହିତ ନିୟମିତ ପ୍ରସବ ପୂର୍ବ ଯତ୍ନ ଜାରି ରଖନ୍ତୁ।

ରିପୋର୍ଟ ପ୍ରସ୍ତୁତ କରିଛନ୍ତି: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Patnaik]]

payload.generated_text_en:
Lab Report — District Hospital Jajapur District [[DISTRICT|Jajapur]]
MRN [[MRN|JH-2024-0815-001]] Patient ID [[PATIENT_ID|JH-2024-0815-001]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Sushila Devi]] [[AGE|25]] [[GENDER|Female]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9438765432]]
Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Patnaik]]
Date: 15 August 2024

Patient Details:
25-year-old female, G2P1, 28 weeks gestation, regular antenatal check-up. Last menstrual period: 15 January 2024. Blood group: O positive. No known allergies.

Laboratory Investigations:
Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL (Normal: 11.0-13.5)
White Blood Cell Count: 7,200/mm³ (Normal: 4,000-11,000)
Platelet Count: 210,000/mm³ (Normal: 150,000-450,000)
Blood Sugar (Fasting): 85 mg/dL (Normal: 70-100)
Blood Sugar (Postprandial): 115 mg/dL (Normal: <140)
Urine Protein: Trace (Negative for proteinuria)
Urine Sugar: Negative
Urine Routine: Normal
Blood Group: O Positive
Rh Factor: Positive
Ultrasound: Single live fetus, estimated fetal weight 1.1 kg, amniotic fluid index normal, placenta anterior.

Interpretation:
Mild anemia present, likely physiological. No evidence of gestational diabetes. No proteinuria to suggest preeclampsia. Ultrasound findings consistent with appropriate fetal growth for 28 weeks. Continue routine antenatal care with iron supplementation.

Report generated by: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Patnaik]]
[[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Jajapur]]

payload.professional_persona: Anuprabha Majumdar, a 27‑year‑old agro‑entrepreneur from the fertile floodplains, blends curiosity‑driven experimentation with meticulous record‑keeping to improve paddy and mustard yields, leverages mobile market‑price apps for better price discovery, and mentors fellow women farmers through the local cooperative.
payload.linguistic_persona: Anuprabha Majumdar speaks Assamese fluently and writes it confidently, uses functional English for market negotiations and radio listening, and understands Hindi well enough for official communications, reflecting her organized approach to language learning.
payload.sports_persona: Anuprabha Majumdar follows cricket passionately, cheering for the regional Ranji team and the Indian national side, participates in weekly village kabaddi matches, and practices sunrise yoga to stay flexible and manage stress, though she sometimes skips sessions after a long day in the fields.
payload.arts_persona: Anuprabha Majumdar enjoys listening to folk Bihu songs, especially those sung by Zubeen Garg, watches Bollywood films starring Ranbir Kapoor, partakes in traditional embroidery, and occasionally knits scarves during monsoon evenings, reflecting her blend of modern curiosity and respect for heritage.
payload.travel_persona: Anuprabha Majumdar prefers short weekend trips to nearby tea garden estates and riverbank picnics, dreams of trekking in Darjeeling and exploring the Sundarbans, plans trips meticulously using budget apps, yet often postpones travel during peak farming seasons to meet family responsibilities.
payload.culinary_persona: Anuprabha Majumdar favors tangy masor tenga made with mustard oil, steaming pitha stuffed with coconut, and seasonal mustard greens, enjoys picking fresh bamboo shoots for home cooking, has moderate cooking skills, and often chooses a simple boiled fish over restaurant meals when time is tight.
payload.persona: Anuprabha Majumdar is a methodical agro‑entrepreneur who merges traditional farming with digital tools, worries about market swings, and unwinds by dancing Bihu after a long day.
payload.cultural_background: Anuprabha Majumdar hails from Nagaon, a fertile district in Assam’s heartland. She belongs to a traditional Assamese farming family where rice paddies and mustard fields have shaped daily life for generations. The rural setting weaves together the Assamese language, Bihu celebrations, folk music, and a deep connection to the Brahmaputra floodplains. While rooted in agrarian customs, she also engages with the broader Indian cultural tapestry through regional festivals, market exchanges, and inter‑community ties.
payload.linguistic_background: She grew up speaking Asamiya (Assamese) as her first language and can read and write the Assamese script with confidence. Through her middle‑school education she acquired functional English, sufficient for basic correspondence, market negotiations and radio listening. Hindi, learned as a second language, enables her to understand official communications and interact with traders from other states. Her literacy is solid in Assamese and Hindi, while her English proficiency remains conversational, reflecting her organized and methodical approach to learning.
payload.skills_and_expertise: She is proficient in crop planning for paddy, mustard, and seasonal vegetables, applying both ancestral wisdom and simple modern techniques. Her expertise includes preparing organic fertilizers from farm waste, managing pests using neem‑based solutions, and providing basic livestock care for cows and goats. She maintains farm accounts, tracks expenses, and negotiates market prices with traders, while also coordinating with the local farmers’ cooperative for seed supply and collective marketing. Her methodical nature ensures accurate record‑keeping and timely execution of farm tasks.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Crop planning", "Organic fertiliser preparation", "Pest management (Neem-based)", "Livestock care", "Farm accounting", "Market negotiation", "Cooperative coordination"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: She enjoys participating in Bihu dance during the spring and autumn festivals, cooking traditional Assamese dishes such as masor tenga and pitha, listening to folk music, and reading devotional poetry. Gardening, especially growing medicinal herbs, and occasional knitting are also part of her routine. In her leisure time, she watches regional TV serials and shares tea with neighbours, balancing social interaction with moments of quiet reflection.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Bihu dance", "Cooking Assamese cuisine", "Listening to folk music", "Reading devotional poetry", "Herb gardening", "Knitting", "Watching TV serials", "Tea with neighbours"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Anuprabha’s short‑term goal is to boost the yield and profitability of her family’s paddy and mustard fields by adopting sustainable, organic practices and using mobile market‑price apps for better price discovery. In the medium term, she aims to establish a small agro‑processing unit that produces mustard oil and pickles, creating an additional income stream for her household and providing training opportunities for other women in the village. Ultimately, she aspires to become a respected member of the local farmers’ cooperative, influencing policies that support small‑holder resilience and promoting community‑wide adoption of climate‑smart agriculture.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 27-year-old female cultivator from rural Assam, currently married, with middle education. She speaks Assamese and has functional English, and works as a general cultivator.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Assamese (code=as, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Assam, district=Nagaon.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Bengali for Assamese.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 27
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Cultivator, General
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Assam
- district: Nagaon
- first_language: Assamese
- clinical_persona_summary: A 27-year-old female cultivator from rural Assam, currently married, with middle education. She speaks Assamese and has functional English, and works as a general cultivator.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.professional_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old community culinary and handloom mentor, channels their curiosity and practicality into mastering traditional vegetable gardening, sweet preparation and hand‑woven saree crafting, organizing village festivals and uplifting women’s self‑help groups with a reliably compassionate and sociable approach.
payload.linguistic_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old native Odia speaker, communicates fluently in their mother tongue, understands basic Hindi from radio and television, and picks up occasional English words like ‘school’ and ‘doctor’, but remains illiterate in all languages.
payload.sports_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old energetic participant in village fitness circles, enjoys daily walks in their vegetable garden, practices yoga with the local women's yoga group, watches and cheers for the Kalahandi Cricket Club in regional matches, and occasionally joins friendly kabaddi games during festive gatherings.
payload.arts_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old folk song enthusiast, routinely sings traditional ballads like ‘Phula Rachana’, dances spiritedly at festivals, listens avidly to elder storytellers of mythological epics, and draws artistic inspiration from performers such as the renowned Odissi dancer Sonal Mansingh and folk vocalist Gopal Chandra.
payload.travel_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old modest traveler, enjoys short trips to nearby Puri during Rath Yatra, visits Bhubaneswar’s weekly market for spices, dreams of a pilgrimage to Varanasi and a cultural tour of Kolkata, but plans each journey carefully within their limited savings and family responsibilities.
payload.culinary_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old culinary aficionado, delights in preparing and savoring traditional dishes such as pakhala with fermented rice, tangy fish curry with mustard seeds, sweet chhena poda, arisa, and soft rasgulla, and prefers ingredients like mustard, poppy seeds and fresh river fish, often sharing these meals with family and neighbors during festivals.
payload.persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old village matriarch, blends bright folk singing, deft handloom weaving, and herbal home remedies with a perpetual curiosity, compassionate teamwork, and a disciplined savings habit, all while juggling limited literacy and a lively social circle.
payload.cultural_background: Malti grew up in the rural village of Nabarangapur in the southern part of Odisha, where Odia culture is deeply rooted in agrarian life, tribal influences, and Hindu traditions. Her family follows the customs of joint households, respects elders, and participates in regional festivals such as Raja Parba, Makar Sankranti, and the grand Rath Yatra of Puri. The everyday rhythm of village life, from morning prayers at the home shrine to communal feasts, shapes her sense of belonging and cultural identity.
payload.linguistic_background: Malti's mother tongue is Odia, which she speaks fluently and uses for all daily communication. Because she never attended school, she cannot read or write in any language, including Odia or Hindi. She can understand basic Hindi spoken on radio and television, and she occasionally picks up simple English words like 'school' or 'doctor' from community interactions, but she does not converse in English. Her linguistic competence is thus strong oral Odia, limited comprehension of Hindi, and no literacy.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Malti excels at traditional Odia cooking, especially preparing regional dishes like pakhala, rasgulla, and fish curries. She is skilled in handloom weaving, creating sarees and scarves for her family. Her expertise includes efficient home management, organizing community events, using herbal remedies for common ailments, maintaining a small vegetable garden, and preserving folk songs and stories by narrating them to younger generations.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Traditional Odia cooking', 'Handloom weaving', 'Home management', 'Community event coordination', 'Herbal home remedies', 'Gardening', 'Storytelling and folk songs']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Malti enjoys singing traditional Odia folk songs during evenings, dancing at festivals, tending to her vegetable garden, preparing sweets like arisa and chhena poda, attending village fairs, and listening to elders recount mythological stories.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Folk singing', 'Festive dancing', 'Gardening', 'Traditional sweet making', 'Attending village fairs', 'Listening to mythological stories']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Malti hopes to pass her culinary and weaving knowledge to her grandchildren, ensuring they appreciate Odia heritage. She wishes to support local women's self-help groups by sharing her expertise in household budgeting and traditional crafts. Additionally, she aspires to learn basic reading and writing in Odia through community literacy programs, so she can keep records of family recipes and festival schedules.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Malti Odia is a 59-year-old female from Nabarangapur, Odisha, currently married and retired. She is illiterate, speaks Odia, and lives in a rural area. Her lifestyle includes traditional handloom weaving, herbal home remedies, and a disciplined savings habit.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]

  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]

  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]

  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]

  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Odia (code=or, script=Odia). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Nabarangapur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Odia for Odia.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 59
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Nabarangapur
- first_language: Odia
- clinical_persona_summary: Malti Odia is a 59-year-old female from Nabarangapur, Odisha, currently married and retired. She is illiterate, speaks Odia, and lives in a rural area. Her lifestyle includes traditional handloom weaving, herbal home remedies, and a disciplined savings habit.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ବର୍ତ୍ତମାନର ରୋଗର ଇତିହାସ: ରୋଗୀ ଗତ ଦୁଇ ସପ୍ତାହ ଧରି, ବିଶେଷ କରି ସକାଳେ, କ୍ରମାଗତ ଦୁର୍ବଳତା ବିଷୟରେ ଜଣାଇଛନ୍ତି। ଆଣ୍ଠୁ ଏବଂ ହାତର ଗଣ୍ଠିରେ ଯନ୍ତ୍ରଣା, ଯାହାକୁ ମାନ୍ଦା ଯନ୍ତ୍ରଣା ଭାବରେ ବର୍ଣ୍ଣନା କରାଯାଇଛି। ବେଳେବେଳେ କପାଳ ମୁଣ୍ଡବିନ୍ଧା, ଯାହା ଅପରାହ୍ନରେ ଅଧିକ ହୁଏ। ଜ୍ୱର, ଓଜନ ହ୍ରାସ କିମ୍ବା ରାତିରେ ଝାଳ ବୋହିବା ନାହିଁ। ଆଂଶିକ ଆରାମ ପାଇଁ ବେଳେବେଳେ ପାରାସିଟାମୋଲ୍ ସେବନ କରୁଛନ୍ତି।

ପରୀକ୍ଷା:
ଜୀବନ ରକ୍ଷାକାରୀ ସଙ୍କେତ - BP 130/85 mmHg, Pulse 78/min, Temp 98.2°F, SpO2 98% room air ରେ।
ସାଧାରଣ - ଫିକା, ସାମାନ୍ୟ ଜଳଶୂନ୍ୟତା।
ହୃଦ୍‌ରୋଗ ସମ୍ବନ୍ଧୀୟ - ନିୟମିତ ତାଳ, କୌଣସି ମର୍ମର୍ ଶବ୍ଦ ନାହିଁ।
ଶ୍ୱାସକ୍ରିୟା - ଉଭୟ ପାର୍ଶ୍ୱରେ ସ୍ପଷ୍ଟ।
ଉଦର - ନରମ, ଯନ୍ତ୍ରଣାହୀନ।
ମାଂସପେଶୀ ଏବଂ କଙ୍କାଳ - ଉଭୟ ଆଣ୍ଠୁ ଗଣ୍ଠିରେ ସାମାନ୍ୟ ଫୁଲା ଏବଂ କୋମଳତା।
ସ୍ନାୟୁଗତ - ସତର୍କ, ସଚେତନ, କୌଣସି ନିର୍ଦ୍ଦିଷ୍ଟ ଅଭାବ ନାହିଁ।

ପରୀକ୍ଷା:
CBC - Hb 9.8 g/dL, WBC 7,200/cu mm, platelets 2.1 lakh/cu mm.
ESR - 45 mm/hr.
ରକ୍ତ ଶର୍କରା - ଖାଲି ପେଟରେ 110 mg/dL, PP 165 mg/dL.

ନିଦାନ:
1. ରକ୍ତହୀନତା
2. ଅଷ୍ଟିଓଆର୍ଥ୍ରାଇଟିସ୍
3. ଚାପ ସମ୍ବନ୍ଧୀୟ ମୁଣ୍ଡବିନ୍ଧା

ଚିକିତ୍ସା:
1. 3 ମାସ ପାଇଁ ପ୍ରତିଦିନ ଆଇରନ୍ ଏବଂ ଫୋଲିକ୍ ଏସିଡ୍ ଟାବଲେଟ୍
2. ଯନ୍ତ୍ରଣା ପାଇଁ ପାରାସିଟାମୋଲ୍ 500mg TDS
3. ଗଣ୍ଠି ପାଇଁ ଉଷୁମ ସେକ
4. ପର୍ଯ୍ୟାପ୍ତ ଜଳପାନ ଏବଂ ପୁଷ୍ଟିକର ଖାଦ୍ୟ

ଅନୁବର୍ତ୍ତନ:
4 ସପ୍ତାହ ପରେ ସମୀକ୍ଷା କରନ୍ତୁ। CBC ଏବଂ ESR ପୁନରାବୃତ୍ତି କରନ୍ତୁ। ଯଦି ଗଣ୍ଠି ଯନ୍ତ୍ରଣା ଜାରି ରହେ, ତେବେ ଅର୍ଥୋପେଡିକ୍ ବିଭାଗକୁ ପଠାନ୍ତୁ।

ପ୍ରଦତ୍ତ ପ୍ରେସକ୍ରିପସନ୍:
- ଆଇରନ୍ ଏବଂ ଫୋଲିକ୍ ଏସିଡ୍ (ଦିନକୁ 1 ଟାବଲେଟ୍)
- ପାରାସିଟାମୋଲ୍ (500mg, ଦିନକୁ 3 ଟାବଲେଟ୍)
- ଭିଟାମିନ୍ B12 ଇଞ୍ଜେକ୍ସନ (1000mcg, ଏକକ ମାତ୍ରା)
payload.generated_text_en:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Nabarangapur]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-NAB-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Malti]] | DOB [[DOB|1965-03-15]] | Age: [[AGE|59]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Homemaker]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0815-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. S. Patnaik]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9437812345]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-2024-001]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Nabarangapur]]
Chief complaint: Generalized body weakness, joint pain, and occasional headaches for 2 weeks.

History of present illness: Patient reports progressive weakness over the past two weeks, particularly in the mornings. Joint pain affecting knees and hands, described as dull aching. Occasional frontal headaches, worse in afternoon. No fever, weight loss, or night sweats. Taking occasional paracetamol with partial relief.

Examination: Vitals - BP 130/85 mmHg, Pulse 78/min, Temp 98.2°F, SpO2 98% on room air. General - Pale, mildly dehydrated. Cardiovascular - Regular rhythm, no murmurs. Respiratory - Clear bilaterally. Abdomen - Soft, non-tender. Musculoskeletal - Mild swelling and tenderness in bilateral knee joints. Neurological - Alert, oriented, no focal deficits.

Investigations: CBC - Hb 9.8 g/dL, WBC 7,200/cu mm, platelets 2.1 lakh/cu mm. ESR - 45 mm/hr. Blood sugar - Fasting 110 mg/dL, PP 165 mg/dL.

Diagnosis: 1. Anemia 2. Osteoarthritis 3. Stress-related headaches

Treatment: 1. Iron and folic acid tablets daily for 3 months 2. Paracetamol 500mg TDS for pain 3. Warm compresses for joints 4. Adequate hydration and nutrition

Follow-up: Review in 4 weeks. Repeat CBC and ESR. Referral to orthopedic if joint pain persists.

Prescriptions given:
- Iron & Folic Acid (1 tablet daily)
- Paracetamol (500mg, 3 tablets daily)
- Vitamin B12 injection (1000mcg, single dose)

payload.professional_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old community culinary and handloom mentor, channels their curiosity and practicality into mastering traditional vegetable gardening, sweet preparation and hand‑woven saree crafting, organizing village festivals and uplifting women’s self‑help groups with a reliably compassionate and sociable approach.
payload.linguistic_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old native Odia speaker, communicates fluently in their mother tongue, understands basic Hindi from radio and television, and picks up occasional English words like ‘school’ and ‘doctor’, but remains illiterate in all languages.
payload.sports_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old energetic participant in village fitness circles, enjoys daily walks in their vegetable garden, practices yoga with the local women's yoga group, watches and cheers for the Kalahandi Cricket Club in regional matches, and occasionally joins friendly kabaddi games during festive gatherings.
payload.arts_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old folk song enthusiast, routinely sings traditional ballads like ‘Phula Rachana’, dances spiritedly at festivals, listens avidly to elder storytellers of mythological epics, and draws artistic inspiration from performers such as the renowned Odissi dancer Sonal Mansingh and folk vocalist Gopal Chandra.
payload.travel_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old modest traveler, enjoys short trips to nearby Puri during Rath Yatra, visits Bhubaneswar’s weekly market for spices, dreams of a pilgrimage to Varanasi and a cultural tour of Kolkata, but plans each journey carefully within their limited savings and family responsibilities.
payload.culinary_persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old culinary aficionado, delights in preparing and savoring traditional dishes such as pakhala with fermented rice, tangy fish curry with mustard seeds, sweet chhena poda, arisa, and soft rasgulla, and prefers ingredients like mustard, poppy seeds and fresh river fish, often sharing these meals with family and neighbors during festivals.
payload.persona: Malti Odia, a 59‑year‑old village matriarch, blends bright folk singing, deft handloom weaving, and herbal home remedies with a perpetual curiosity, compassionate teamwork, and a disciplined savings habit, all while juggling limited literacy and a lively social circle.
payload.cultural_background: Malti grew up in the rural village of Nabarangapur in the southern part of Odisha, where Odia culture is deeply rooted in agrarian life, tribal influences, and Hindu traditions. Her family follows the customs of joint households, respects elders, and participates in regional festivals such as Raja Parba, Makar Sankranti, and the grand Rath Yatra of Puri. The everyday rhythm of village life, from morning prayers at the home shrine to communal feasts, shapes her sense of belonging and cultural identity.
payload.linguistic_background: Malti's mother tongue is Odia, which she speaks fluently and uses for all daily communication. Because she never attended school, she cannot read or write in any language, including Odia or Hindi. She can understand basic Hindi spoken on radio and television, and she occasionally picks up simple English words like 'school' or 'doctor' from community interactions, but she does not converse in English. Her linguistic competence is thus strong oral Odia, limited comprehension of Hindi, and no literacy.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Malti excels at traditional Odia cooking, especially preparing regional dishes like pakhala, rasgulla, and fish curries. She is skilled in handloom weaving, creating sarees and scarves for her family. Her expertise includes efficient home management, organizing community events, using herbal remedies for common ailments, maintaining a small vegetable garden, and preserving folk songs and stories by narrating them to younger generations.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Traditional Odia cooking', 'Handloom weaving', 'Home management', 'Community event coordination', 'Herbal home remedies', 'Gardening', 'Storytelling and folk songs']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Malti enjoys singing traditional Odia folk songs during evenings, dancing at festivals, tending to her vegetable garden, preparing sweets like arisa and chhena poda, attending village fairs, and listening to elders recount mythological stories.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Folk singing', 'Festive dancing', 'Gardening', 'Traditional sweet making', 'Attending village fairs', 'Listening to mythological stories']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Malti hopes to pass her culinary and weaving knowledge to her grandchildren, ensuring they appreciate Odia heritage. She wishes to support local women's self-help groups by sharing her expertise in household budgeting and traditional crafts. Additionally, she aspires to learn basic reading and writing in Odia through community literacy programs, so she can keep records of family recipes and festival schedules.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Malti Odia, a 59-year-old female from Nabarangapur, Odisha, is a retired homemaker with an illiterate education level living in a rural zone. She speaks Odia as her first language and has limited exposure to Hindi. Her lifestyle includes traditional handloom weaving, folk singing, and herbal home remedies, with a disciplined savings habit.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]

  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]

  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]

  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]

  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]

  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Odia (code=or, script=Odia). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Nabarangapur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Odia for Odia.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 59
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
