payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Discharge Summary for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Telugu (code=te, script=Telugu). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Nanded.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Telugu for Telugu.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Nanded
- first_language: Telugu
- clinical_persona_summary: An 18‑year‑old male from Nanded, Maharashtra, who is never married, has completed primary education, and lives in an urban zone. He speaks Telugu as his first language, with functional Marathi and basic Hindi proficiency. His lifestyle includes sketching mythic tales and occasional street cricket, with a diet favoring spicy Telugu foods like Pesarattu and vada pav.

Document format guide for Discharge Summary (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] DOB [[DOB|1994-06-21]] [[AGE|30]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]] | Course / advice: <concise paragraphs matching domain + persona sex/age>

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ABHA_ADDRESS — ABHA Address e.g. [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
- CASTE — Caste e.g. [[CASTE|OBC]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Ravi Kumar]] DOB [[DOB|2006-03-15]] [[AGE|18]] [[GENDER|Male]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0315-001]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|A1]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|05]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]] | Course / advice: Patient presented with acute gastroenteritis characterized by watery diarrhea, vomiting, and abdominal cramps. Diet should be light and easily digestible, avoiding spicy and oily foods. Patient and his father [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]] have been counseled on hand hygiene and safe food practices. [[DISTRICT|Nanded]] [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] [[ABHA_ADDRESS|ravi.kumar@abdm]] [[CASTE|OBC]]

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]

  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]

  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Referral Letter for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Telugu (code=te, script=Telugu). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Nanded.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Telugu for Telugu.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Nanded
- first_language: Telugu
- clinical_persona_summary: An 18-year-old male from Nanded, Maharashtra, who is never married, retired, and has primary education. He speaks Telugu as his first language and lives in an urban zone. Clinically, he presents as a visual storyteller with a relaxed, often disorganized schedule, and his lifestyle includes occasional street cricket and a diet rich in spicy Telugu fare. He is multilingual, with functional Marathi and basic Hindi skills.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|36]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Reason: <domain-appropriate clinical reason matching persona>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- REFERRAL_ID — Referral ID e.g. [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PASSPORT_NUMBER — Passport Number e.g. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815-001]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government Medical College Hospital Nanded]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ravi Kumar]], [[AGE|18]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|Nanded]]
Reason: Acute appendicitis requiring surgical intervention

Dear Colleague,

This is to refer [[PATIENT_NAME|Ravi Kumar]], an 18-year-old male patient from Nanded, for surgical evaluation and management of acute appendicitis. The patient presented to our emergency department with right lower quadrant abdominal pain, fever, and vomiting for the past 24 hours.

On examination, the patient has tenderness at McBurney's point with rebound tenderness and guarding. Laboratory investigations show leukocytosis with neutrophilia. Ultrasound abdomen confirms acute appendicitis with diameter of 8mm and surrounding fluid collection.

The patient's [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] is attached for your reference. His [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] is available for any clarifications. The patient's father [[RELATIVE_NAME|Srinivas Kumar]] has been informed and is accompanying him.

Patient's [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P1234567]] and [[DRIVING_LICENCE|MH042024001234]] are attached for identification purposes.

We request you to evaluate the patient for laparoscopic appendectomy at your earliest convenience. The patient is otherwise healthy with no significant comorbidities.

Yours sincerely,
Dr. Rajesh Kumar
General Surgeon
Government Medical College Hospital Nanded

payload.professional_persona: Bibhuti Raut is a meticulous window cleaner who blends physical stamina with an artist’s eye for detail, follows strict ladder safety protocols, plans daily routes methodically, engages politely with customers, and they are known for their organized and curious nature.
payload.linguistic_persona: Bibhuti Raut speaks Telugu at a primary‑school level, converses fluently in functional Odia for everyday tasks, and understands spoken Hindi, though reading and writing in Hindi remain limited, and they switch between languages with ease.
payload.sports_persona: Bibhuti Raut enjoys playing cricket with friends at the local ground, cheers for the Chennai Super Kings in the IPL, follows the Odisha women's football team, and they treat weekly matches as a key way to stay fit and socially connected.
payload.arts_persona: Bibhuti Raut immerses in Telugu film songs by S. P. Balasubrahmanyam and Odia folk classics like “Rangabati”, sketches simple line drawings of temple arches during idle moments, and they attend community painting workshops whenever possible, letting their curiosity for art brighten their routine.
payload.travel_persona: Bibhuti Raut prefers short weekend trips to nearby coastal spots like Puri and Konark, plans travel via affordable train tickets, enjoys simple guesthouse stays, dreams of visiting Tirupati for a pilgrimage and hopes to someday explore Bangkok’s markets when finances allow, and they hope to someday explore Bangkok’s markets when finances allow.
payload.culinary_persona: Bibhuti Raut enjoys hearty meals of rice, fish curry seasoned with mustard oil and tamarind, savors Odia staples like pakhala and rasgulla, relishes Telugu biryani with a side of raita, and often indulges in street‑side puchka, though they rarely cook at home.
payload.persona: Bibhuti Raut is a diligent window cleaner whose artistic curiosity fuels a love for music, sketching and cricket, while their organized mindset and modest frugality keep them grounded, and they stay humble in every task.
payload.cultural_background: Bibhuti Raut hails from a Telugu‑speaking family that settled in the coastal district of Ganjam, Odisha. Growing up in an urban environment, he blends the cultural practices of his Telugu roots—celebrating festivals like Ugadi and Sankranti, enjoying Carnatic music and traditional dance—with the local Odia customs he encounters daily. As a member of the Raut community, he identifies with modest, working‑class values and a strong sense of communal solidarity.
payload.linguistic_background: His mother tongue is Telugu, which he can speak, read, and write at a primary‑school level. Living in Ganjam, he has picked up functional Odia for everyday communication, though he is not formally literate in it. He also understands spoken Hindi from television and local interactions, but his reading and writing skills in Hindi are limited.
payload.skills_and_expertise: His primary expertise lies in window cleaning, where he combines physical stamina with meticulous attention to detail. He is adept at using ladders and safety gear, follows high‑rise safety protocols, and performs routine maintenance of cleaning equipment. Organized and methodical, he plans his daily routes efficiently, manages time well, and ensures consistent service quality. Bibhuti's curiosity about art sharpens his observational skills, enabling him to notice even subtle dust patterns and surface imperfections. He also possesses good interpersonal skills for brief customer interactions, and basic troubleshooting abilities for minor tool repairs.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Window cleaning", "High‑rise safety protocol compliance", "Use of ladders and safety gear", "Routine equipment maintenance", "Time management", "Route planning", "Attention to detail", "Observational skills", "Customer interaction", "Basic tool troubleshooting"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Bibhuti enjoys listening to Telugu film songs and Odia folk music, which fuel his love for rhythm and melody. He often watches regional movies and occasionally sketches simple drawings inspired by the cityscape and temple architecture he encounters during his work. Playing cricket with friends in local grounds, taking evening walks to observe street art, and reading short stories in Telugu are his favorite ways to unwind. He also has a keen interest in learning basic painting techniques through community art workshops whenever time permits.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Telugu and Odia music", "Watching regional movies", "Sketching cityscapes and temple architecture", "Playing cricket with friends", "Evening walks observing street art", "Reading Telugu short stories", "Participating in community art workshops"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Bibhuti aims to move beyond his current role as a window cleaner by acquiring formal certification in occupational safety and building maintenance. Within the next few years, he aspires to become a supervisor of a cleaning crew, allowing him to oversee larger projects and mentor junior workers. In the longer term, he dreams of starting his own small cleaning service business, which would provide a steadier income for his family and enable him to fund further studies in a vocational trade such as carpentry or plumbing. He also hopes to deepen his appreciation for art by attending community workshops whenever possible.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Male, 21, never married, works as a cleaner and window cleaner, with primary education, living in an urban area of Ganjam district, Odisha. Telugu is his first language, with functional Odia skills. He is modest and frugal, and his lifestyle includes daily physical activity and limited alcohol consumption.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Hospital Billing / Invoice for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Telugu (code=te, script=Telugu). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Ganjam.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Telugu for Telugu.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once (parking/ambulance or motor claim line). Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 21
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Cleaner, Window
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Odisha
- district: Ganjam
- first_language: Telugu
- clinical_persona_summary: Male, 21, never married, works as a cleaner and window cleaner, with primary education, living in an urban area of Ganjam district, Odisha. Telugu is his first language, with functional Odia skills. He is modest and frugal, and his lifestyle includes daily physical activity and limited alcohol consumption.

Document format guide for Hospital Billing / Invoice (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (English pivot; wrap facility/geo as tags — never plain-only):

PIN [[PIN_CODE|713101]] Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] MRN [[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815]]

payload.professional_persona: Bibhuti Raut is a meticulous window cleaner who blends physical stamina with an artist’s eye for detail, follows strict ladder safety protocols, plans daily routes methodically, engages politely with customers, and they are known for their organized and curious nature.
payload.linguistic_persona: Bibhuti Raut speaks Telugu at a primary‑school level, converses fluently in functional Odia for everyday tasks, and understands spoken Hindi, though reading and writing in Hindi remain limited, and they switch between languages with ease.
payload.sports_persona: Bibhuti Raut enjoys playing cricket with friends at the local ground, cheers for the Chennai Super Kings in the IPL, follows the Odisha women's football team, and they treat weekly matches as a key way to stay fit and socially connected.
payload.arts_persona: Bibhuti Raut immerses in Telugu film songs by S. P. Balasubrahmanyam and Odia folk classics like “Rangabati”, sketches simple line drawings of temple arches during idle moments, and they attend community painting workshops whenever possible, letting their curiosity for art brighten their routine.
payload.travel_persona: Bibhuti Raut prefers short weekend trips to nearby coastal spots like Puri and Konark, plans travel via affordable train tickets, enjoys simple guesthouse stays, dreams of visiting Tirupati for a pilgrimage and hopes to someday explore Bangkok’s markets when finances allow, and they hope to someday explore Bangkok’s markets when finances allow.
payload.culinary_persona: Bibhuti Raut enjoys hearty meals of rice, fish curry seasoned with mustard oil and tamarind, savors Odia staples like pakhala and rasgulla, relishes Telugu biryani with a side of raita, and often indulges in street‑side puchka, though they rarely cook at home.
payload.persona: Bibhuti Raut is a diligent window cleaner whose artistic curiosity fuels a love for music, sketching and cricket, while their organized mindset and modest frugality keep them grounded, and they stay humble in every task.
payload.cultural_background: Bibhuti Raut hails from a Telugu‑speaking family that settled in the coastal district of Ganjam, Odisha. Growing up in an urban environment, he blends the cultural practices of his Telugu roots—celebrating festivals like Ugadi and Sankranti, enjoying Carnatic music and traditional dance—with the local Odia customs he encounters daily. As a member of the Raut community, he identifies with modest, working‑class values and a strong sense of communal solidarity.
payload.linguistic_background: His mother tongue is Telugu, which he can speak, read, and write at a primary‑school level. Living in Ganjam, he has picked up functional Odia for everyday communication, though he is not formally literate in it. He also understands spoken Hindi from television and local interactions, but his reading and writing skills in Hindi are limited.
payload.skills_and_expertise: His primary expertise lies in window cleaning, where he combines physical stamina with meticulous attention to detail. He is adept at using ladders and safety gear, follows high‑rise safety protocols, and performs routine maintenance of cleaning equipment. Organized and methodical, he plans his daily routes efficiently, manages time well, and ensures consistent service quality. Bibhuti's curiosity about art sharpens his observational skills, enabling him to notice even subtle dust patterns and surface imperfections. He also possesses good interpersonal skills for brief customer interactions, and basic troubleshooting abilities for minor tool repairs.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Window cleaning", "High‑rise safety protocol compliance", "Use of ladders and safety gear", "Routine equipment maintenance", "Time management", "Route planning", "Attention to detail", "Observational skills", "Customer interaction", "Basic tool troubleshooting"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Bibhuti enjoys listening to Telugu film songs and Odia folk music, which fuel his love for rhythm and melody. He often watches regional movies and occasionally sketches simple drawings inspired by the cityscape and temple architecture he encounters during his work. Playing cricket with friends in local grounds, taking evening walks to observe street art, and reading short stories in Telugu are his favorite ways to unwind. He also has a keen interest in learning basic painting techniques through community art workshops whenever time permits.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Telugu and Odia music", "Watching regional movies", "Sketching cityscapes and temple architecture", "Playing cricket with friends", "Evening walks observing street art", "Reading Telugu short stories", "Participating in community art workshops"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Bibhuti aims to move beyond his current role as a window cleaner by acquiring formal certification in occupational safety and building maintenance. Within the next few years, he aspires to become a supervisor of a cleaning crew, allowing him to oversee larger projects and mentor junior workers. In the longer term, he dreams of starting his own small cleaning service business, which would provide a steadier income for his family and enable him to fund further studies in a vocational trade such as carpentry or plumbing. He also hopes to deepen his appreciation for art by attending community workshops whenever possible.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Discharge Summary for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Telugu (code=te, script=Telugu). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Ganjam.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Telugu for Telugu.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 21
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Cleaner, Window
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Odisha
- district: Ganjam
- first_language: Telugu
- clinical_persona_summary: Male, 21, never married, cleaner, window, primary education, urban, Odisha, Ganjam, Telugu. Modest, organized, and frugal, he follows safety protocols and maintains polite customer interactions. Functional Odia for daily use, with cultural roots in Telugu traditions.

Document format guide for Discharge Summary (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] DOB [[DOB|1994-06-21]] [[AGE|30]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]] | Course / advice: <concise paragraphs matching domain + persona sex/age>

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ABHA_ADDRESS — ABHA Address e.g. [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
- CASTE — Caste e.g. [[CASTE|OBC]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]] DOB [[DOB|2003-04-15]] [[AGE|21]] [[GENDER|Male]]

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sanjay Patnaik]] | Course / advice: Patient presented with persistent cough for 3 weeks, low-grade fever, and weight loss of 5 kg. Started on DOTS therapy with isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol. Patient also has hypertension noted on admission (BP 140/90 mmHg), likely secondary to stress. Discharged on 14th day of treatment with continuation of anti-tubercular therapy for total 6 months. Advised to complete full course, maintain good nutrition, and avoid smoking. Follow-up scheduled in 2 weeks at TB clinic. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sita Devi]] (Mother) informed about treatment plan. [[DISTRICT|Ganjam]] [[PHONE_NUMBER|9438912345]] [[ABHA_ADDRESS|ramesh.kumar@abdm]] [[CASTE|SC]]

payload.professional_persona: Bibhuti Raut is a meticulous window cleaner who blends physical stamina with an artist’s eye for detail, follows strict ladder safety protocols, plans daily routes methodically, engages politely with customers, and they are known for their organized and curious nature.
payload.linguistic_persona: Bibhuti Raut speaks Telugu at a primary‑school level, converses fluently in functional Odia for everyday tasks, and understands spoken Hindi, though reading and writing in Hindi remain limited, and they switch between languages with ease.
payload.sports_persona: Bibhuti Raut enjoys playing cricket with friends at the local ground, cheers for the Chennai Super Kings in the IPL, follows the Odisha women's football team, and they treat weekly matches as a key way to stay fit and socially connected.
payload.arts_persona: Bibhuti Raut immerses in Telugu film songs by S. P. Balasubrahmanyam and Odia folk classics like “Rangabati”, sketches simple line drawings of temple arches during idle moments, and they attend community painting workshops whenever possible, letting their curiosity for art brighten their routine.
payload.travel_persona: Bibhuti Raut prefers short weekend trips to nearby coastal spots like Puri and Konark, plans travel via affordable train tickets, enjoys simple guesthouse stays, dreams of visiting Tirupati for a pilgrimage and hopes to someday explore Bangkok’s markets when finances allow, and they hope to someday explore Bangkok’s markets when finances allow.
payload.culinary_persona: Bibhuti Raut enjoys hearty meals of rice, fish curry seasoned with mustard oil and tamarind, savors Odia staples like pakhala and rasgulla, relishes Telugu biryani with a side of raita, and often indulges in street‑side puchka, though they rarely cook at home.
payload.persona: Bibhuti Raut is a diligent window cleaner whose artistic curiosity fuels a love for music, sketching and cricket, while their organized mindset and modest frugality keep them grounded, and they stay humble in every task.
payload.cultural_background: Bibhuti Raut hails from a Telugu‑speaking family that settled in the coastal district of Ganjam, Odisha. Growing up in an urban environment, he blends the cultural practices of his Telugu roots—celebrating festivals like Ugadi and Sankranti, enjoying Carnatic music and traditional dance—with the local Odia customs he encounters daily. As a member of the Raut community, he identifies with modest, working‑class values and a strong sense of communal solidarity.
payload.linguistic_background: His mother tongue is Telugu, which he can speak, read, and write at a primary‑school level. Living in Ganjam, he has picked up functional Odia for everyday communication, though he is not formally literate in it. He also understands spoken Hindi from television and local interactions, but his reading and writing skills in Hindi are limited.
payload.skills_and_expertise: His primary expertise lies in window cleaning, where he combines physical stamina with meticulous attention to detail. He is adept at using ladders and safety gear, follows high‑rise safety protocols, and performs routine maintenance of cleaning equipment. Organized and methodical, he plans his daily routes efficiently, manages time well, and ensures consistent service quality. Bibhuti's curiosity about art sharpens his observational skills, enabling him to notice even subtle dust patterns and surface imperfections. He also possesses good interpersonal skills for brief customer interactions, and basic troubleshooting abilities for minor tool repairs.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Window cleaning", "High‑rise safety protocol compliance", "Use of ladders and safety gear", "Routine equipment maintenance", "Time management", "Route planning", "Attention to detail", "Observational skills", "Customer interaction", "Basic tool troubleshooting"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Bibhuti enjoys listening to Telugu film songs and Odia folk music, which fuel his love for rhythm and melody. He often watches regional movies and occasionally sketches simple drawings inspired by the cityscape and temple architecture he encounters during his work. Playing cricket with friends in local grounds, taking evening walks to observe street art, and reading short stories in Telugu are his favorite ways to unwind. He also has a keen interest in learning basic painting techniques through community art workshops whenever time permits.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Telugu and Odia music", "Watching regional movies", "Sketching cityscapes and temple architecture", "Playing cricket with friends", "Evening walks observing street art", "Reading Telugu short stories", "Participating in community art workshops"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Bibhuti aims to move beyond his current role as a window cleaner by acquiring formal certification in occupational safety and building maintenance. Within the next few years, he aspires to become a supervisor of a cleaning crew, allowing him to oversee larger projects and mentor junior workers. In the longer term, he dreams of starting his own small cleaning service business, which would provide a steadier income for his family and enable him to fund further studies in a vocational trade such as carpentry or plumbing. He also hopes to deepen his appreciation for art by attending community workshops whenever possible.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Referral Letter for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Telugu (code=te, script=Telugu). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Ganjam.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Telugu for Telugu.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 21
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Cleaner, Window
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Odisha
- district: Ganjam
- first_language: Telugu
- clinical_persona_summary: Male, 21, never married, works as a window cleaner, with primary education. Resides in urban Ganjam, Odisha, and speaks Telugu as first language. Leads a modest lifestyle with limited formal literacy in Odia.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|36]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Reason: <domain-appropriate clinical reason matching persona>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- REFERRAL_ID — Referral ID e.g. [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PASSPORT_NUMBER — Passport Number e.g. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815-001]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SCB Medical College Hospital]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ramesh Patnaik]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Sanjay Kumar]], [[AGE|21]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|Ganjam]]
Reason: Persistent cough and breathlessness for 3 weeks

Dear Sir/Madam,

This is to refer [[PATIENT_NAME|Sanjay Kumar]], a 21-year-old male window cleaner residing in urban Ganjam, Odisha, who presents with complaints of productive cough and progressive dyspnea for the past three weeks. The patient reports occasional fever and generalized body aches.

On examination, vitals show temperature 101.2°F, pulse 98/min, respiratory rate 22/min, and blood pressure 120/80 mmHg. Chest examination reveals bilateral crackles in the lower zones. The patient appears moderately ill with reduced exercise tolerance.

Investigations ordered at our facility include complete blood count, chest X-ray, and sputum examination. Preliminary reports suggest possible lower respiratory tract infection. Given the patient's occupational exposure to dust and heights, and considering the persistent symptoms, we request your expert opinion for further management.

[[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9437891234]]
[[RELATIVE_NAME|Smt. Laxmi Devi]] (Mother)
[[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P1234567]]
[[DRIVING_LICENCE|OD14 20200012345]]

Please find the patient's [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|234567890123]] and [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|sanjay.kumar@email.com]] for future correspondence.

Yours sincerely,
Dr. Ramesh Patnaik
General Medicine Department
SCB Medical College Hospital, Cuttack
