payload.professional_persona: Khema Sonam is a community organizer and household manager whose expertise in budgeting, meticulous meal planning, and coordinating local charity events reflects her methodical, reliable nature and practical, structured approach to everyday challenges.
payload.linguistic_persona: Khema Sonam speaks Urdu fluently, uses Hindi for routine interactions and official matters, and picks up enough Marathi to get by in the neighbourhood, while maintaining functional reading and writing skills in both Urdu and basic Hindi.
payload.sports_persona: At 56, Khema Sonam follows the Mumbai Indians in the IPL, enjoys watching cricket matches on television, participates in a local morning walking group, and practices gentle yoga stretches to stay fit, though she rarely plays any competitive sport herself.
payload.arts_persona: Khema Sonam delights in reciting Ghalib and Faiz verses with lyrical precision, and finds solace in listening to classical Indian instrumental music such as Shivkumar Sharma’s Santoor and Ravi Shankar’s sitar, often weaving these auditory experiences into her daily routines.
payload.travel_persona: Khema Sonam prefers well‑planned weekend getaways to nearby destinations like Lonavala, Shirdi, and Aurangabad, where she can explore historic forts and enjoy simple vegetarian meals, but she hesitates to travel far abroad due to limited digital skills and anxiety about unfamiliar environments.
payload.culinary_persona: Khema Sonam excels at preparing hearty Maharashtrian meals such as varan–bhakri, pithla, and misal, relishes the aroma of toor dal simmered with coconut and peanuts, and also comfortably cooks simple North‑Indian dal and bhaji, though she rarely attempts elaborate gourmet plating.
payload.persona: Khema Sonam is a meticulous, community‑oriented 56‑year‑old who balances frugal household management with a love for Urdu poetry, yet often wrestles with intense anxiety that colors her daily decisions.
payload.cultural_background: Khema Sonam hails from the bustling urban centre of Amravati in Maharashtra, where Marathi, Urdu and Hindi cultures intersect. Raised in a Buddhist family that follows the Ambedkarite tradition, she grew up amidst a mix of Maharashtrian festivals and Buddhist observances, absorbing values of simplicity, discipline and community service. Her cultural outlook is shaped by the practical, trading‑oriented environment of the city and by a household that places a strong emphasis on order, frugality and mutual support.
payload.linguistic_background: Khema’s primary language is Urdu, which she speaks fluently and uses for daily communication at home and in the local market. Hindi is her second language; she understands and converses in it with moderate proficiency, especially when dealing with neighbours and official matters. Her formal schooling ended before primary level, so her reading and writing abilities are functional in Urdu and basic in Hindi, allowing her to manage household records, read medicine labels, and follow Buddhist devotional texts. She also picks up bits of Marathi in the neighbourhood but does not claim full proficiency.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Khema’s expertise lies in efficient household management, including budgeting, meticulous meal planning and preparation of both Maharashtrian and North‑Indian dishes. She possesses basic sewing and embroidery skills, and handles medication schedules for elderly family members with diligence. Her organizational abilities extend to arranging community and Buddhist events, coordinating volunteers, and maintaining clear records. Additionally, she is adept at reciting Urdu poetry and has functional literacy in both Urdu and Hindi, which she uses for simple correspondence and reading devotional texts.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Budgeting", "Meal planning & cooking (Maharashtrian & North Indian)", "Basic sewing & embroidery", "Medication schedule management", "Community & Buddhist event organization", "Volunteer coordination", "Record-keeping", "Urdu poetry recitation", "Functional literacy (Urdu & Hindi)"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Khema enjoys cooking traditional Maharashtrian dishes, especially preparing hearty bhat of dal and bhaji. She finds solace in reciting Urdu poetry, often memorizing verses of Ghalib and Faiz. Gardening small indoor plants, such as pothos and aloe vera, provides her with a calming pastime. She spends time knitting scarves and doing simple embroidery. Regular participation in meditation sessions and Buddhist study circles fulfills her spiritual interest. Organizing local community events, like charity drives during festivals, remains a rewarding activity. She also likes reading easy‑to‑understand health and wellness books and listening to classical Indian instrumental music.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Cooking traditional Maharashtrian dishes", "Reciting Urdu poetry", "Indoor gardening (pothos, aloe vera)", "Knitting scarves", "Simple embroidery", "Meditation and Buddhist study circles", "Organizing community events and charity drives", "Reading health & wellness books", "Listening to classical Indian instrumental music"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Khema aspires to deepen her engagement with Buddhism by facilitating meditation circles for women in her locality and mentoring younger members in the Ambedkarite tradition. She also wishes to document and share her family’s traditional recipes and Urdu poetry with future generations, possibly compiling them into a small booklet. Maintaining her health and emotional well‑being is a priority, so she aims to adopt gentle yoga practice and continue regular meditation to manage her anxiety. Additionally, she hopes to acquire basic digital skills to stay connected with her grandchildren and to assist in community outreach through simple social media platforms.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 56-year-old female from Amravati, Maharashtra, who is currently married and retired, speaks Urdu as her primary language and has below-primary education. She lives in an urban setting and manages household affairs, though she experiences significant anxiety that influences her daily decisions. Her lifestyle includes a preference for local walking and gentle yoga, and she follows the Mumbai Indians in cricket.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Discharge Summary for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Urdu (code=ur, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Amravati.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 56
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Amravati
- first_language: Urdu
- clinical_persona_summary: A 56-year-old female from Amravati, Maharashtra, who is currently married and retired, speaks Urdu as her primary language and has below-primary education. She lives in an urban setting and manages household affairs, though she experiences significant anxiety that influences her daily decisions. Her lifestyle includes a preference for local walking and gentle yoga, and she follows the Mumbai Indians in cricket.

Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] DOB [[DOB|1994-06-21]] [[AGE|30]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]] | Course / advice: <concise paragraphs matching domain + persona sex/age>

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]

- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ABHA_ADDRESS — ABHA Address e.g. [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
- CASTE — Caste e.g. [[CASTE|OBC]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government Medical College Hospital, Amravati]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ayesha Begum]] DOB [[DOB|1968-04-15]] [[AGE|56]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-03-12-0045]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|08]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Patil]] | Course / advice: Patient admitted with pulmonary tuberculosis and comorbid hypertension. Initial sputum examination positive for acid-fast bacilli. Started on standard anti-tubercular therapy (ATT) regimen with isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol. Blood pressure monitored and managed with amlodipine 5mg daily. Patient showed clinical improvement after 2 weeks with decreased cough and improved appetite. Sputum conversion achieved by week 6. Discharged on ATT continuation phase with isoniazid and rifampicin for 4 more months. Counselled on medication adherence and importance of completing full course. payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]

  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Referral Letter for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Urdu (code=ur, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Amravati.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Urdu.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 56
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Amravati
- first_language: Urdu
- clinical_persona_summary: A 56-year-old female from Amravati, Maharashtra, currently married and retired, with below-primary education. She lives in an urban zone and speaks Urdu as her first language, with Hindi as a second language. Her lifestyle includes managing household finances, practicing frugal budgeting, and engaging in community charity work. She experiences anxiety that influences her daily decisions and prefers local cultural activities like Urdu poetry recitation.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|36]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Reason: <domain-appropriate clinical reason matching persona>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- REFERRAL_ID — Referral ID e.g. [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PASSPORT_NUMBER — Passport Number e.g. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government Medical College Hospital Amravati]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Deshmukh]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ayesha Begum]], [[AGE|56]] / [[GENDER|Female]], District [[DISTRICT|Amravati]]
Reason: Persistent hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus requiring specialist consultation

Patient presents with uncontrolled blood pressure readings despite current medication regimen. Blood pressure measurements over the past month have ranged from 160/100 to 170/105 mmHg. Patient reports occasional headaches and mild fatigue. Blood glucose levels have been consistently elevated with fasting readings between 140-160 mg/dL. Patient has a history of hypertension for 8 years and was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus 3 years ago. Current medications include Amlodipine 5mg daily and Metformin 500mg twice daily. Patient reports occasional non-adherence to medication due to anxiety and forgetfulness. Family history is significant for hypertension and diabetes mellitus. Patient is advised to continue current medications and undergo further evaluation at the cardiology department. Patient's husband [[RELATIVE_NAME|Mohammed Iqbal]] will accompany her for the consultation. Patient's [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] and [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] are provided for reference. Patient's [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P1234567]] and [[DRIVING_LICENCE|MH01 20100001234]] are available for identification purposes. Patient is advised to bring all previous medical records and medication lists to the appointment.

She spends time knitting scarves and doing simple embroidery. Regular participation in meditation sessions and Buddhist study circles fulfills her spiritual interest. Organizing local community events, like charity drives during festivals, remains a rewarding activity. She also likes reading easy‑to‑understand health and wellness books and listening to classical Indian instrumental music.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Cooking traditional Maharashtrian dishes", "Reciting Urdu poetry", "Indoor gardening (pothos, aloe vera)", "Knitting scarves", "Simple embroidery", "Meditation and Buddhist study circles", "Organizing community events and charity drives", "Reading health & wellness books", "Listening to classical Indian instrumental music"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Khema aspires to deepen her engagement with Buddhism by facilitating meditation circles for women in her locality and mentoring younger members in the Ambedkarite tradition. She also wishes to document and share her family’s traditional recipes and Urdu poetry with future generations, possibly compiling them into a small booklet. Maintaining her health and emotional well‑being is a priority, so she aims to adopt gentle yoga practice and continue regular meditation to manage her anxiety. Additionally, she hopes to acquire basic digital skills to stay connected with her grandchildren and to assist in community outreach through simple social media platforms.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 56-year-old female from Amravati, Maharashtra, currently married and retired, with below primary education. She lives in an urban zone and speaks Urdu as her first language, with Hindi as a secondary language. Her lifestyle includes managing household finances, practicing yoga, and reciting Urdu poetry, while experiencing anxiety that influences daily decisions.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]

  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Radiology / Imaging Report for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Urdu (code=ur, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Amravati.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Urdu.

PLACEMENT: Include [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] as the reporting radiologist (e.g. Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]]) — do not leave the doctor name as untagged prose.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 56
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Amravati
- first_language: Urdu
- clinical_persona_summary: A 56-year-old female from Amravati, Maharashtra, currently married and retired, with below primary education. She lives in an urban zone and speaks Urdu as her first language, with Hindi as a secondary language. Her lifestyle includes managing household finances, practicing yoga, and reciting Urdu poetry, while experiencing anxiety that influences daily decisions.

Document format guide for Radiology / Imaging Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Findings: <radiology prose, untagged>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text: Radiology Report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Amravati]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|Ayesha Begum]] [[AGE|56]] [[GENDER|Female]] MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Deshmukh]] [[DISTRICT|Amravati]] [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-MH-045]] [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] Findings: Patient presents with acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain for 24 hours. Ultrasound examination reveals an enlarged appendix measuring 8.5cm with appendicolith. Surrounding fat stranding noted. No evidence of perforation. Gallbladder appears normal without stones. Liver and spleen are unremarkable. Bowel loops are mobile without obstruction. Impression: Acute appendicitis without perforation. Surgical consultation recommended.

payload.professional_persona: Rajeshwari Satari, a 22‑year‑old aspiring tiffin‑service entrepreneur, blends practical cooking expertise with hands‑on cash‑book keeping, yet their relaxed approach to planning sometimes causes missed market opportunities.
payload.linguistic_persona: Rajeshwari Satari is fluent in Urdu, reading and writing it effortlessly, but they can only grasp basic spoken Kannada and recognise a few Hindi words from songs, reflecting limited formal literacy.
payload.sports_persona: Rajeshwari Satari enjoys regular walks with her dog across the farmland and occasionally joins informal village cricket matches, cheering for Royal Challengers Bangalore, though they rarely attend a formal gym.
payload.arts_persona: Rajeshwari Satari immerses herself in Urdu ghazals by Mirza Ghalib and the melodious voice of Lata Mangeshkar, knits simple yarn scarves, and hand‑weaves modest cotton fabrics, finding artistic fulfillment both in literature and textile crafts.
payload.travel_persona: Rajeshwari Satari prefers low‑cost weekend trips by state bus to nearby towns like Gulbarga or Bidar, exploring historic forts and local markets, while dreaming of an eventual pilgrimage to Hyderabad’s old city for a ghazal evening.
payload.culinary_persona: Rajeshwari Satari delights in cooking traditional Karnataka dishes such as jolada rotti with sambar, sweet holige, and spiced okra stir‑fry, favouring fresh coriander, coconut, tamarind and jaggery, though they rely mostly on family recipes rather than formal culinary training.
payload.persona: Rajeshwari Satari is a solitary Urdu‑loving aspiring tiffin‑service provider who balances practical cooking and cash‑keeping with a habit of worrying and occasional disorganized impulse buying.
payload.cultural_background: Rajeshwari grew up in a rural village near Gulbarga, Karnataka, where the cultural landscape blends Deccani Urdu traditions with the local Kannada way of life. Her family is Hindu, but Urdu is the language spoken at home, a legacy of the erstwhile Hyderabad State that once covered this region. She celebrates Hindu festivals such as Ugadi and Diwali, while also taking part in community gatherings that feature Urdu poetry recitals. The agrarian setting, joint‑family households, and emphasis on home‑made crafts shape her everyday experience.
payload.linguistic_background: Rajeshwari’s mother tongue is Urdu, and she is fluent in speaking, reading, and writing it in the Perso‑Arabic script. Because she left school before reaching primary level, her formal literacy in Kannada and Hindi is limited to basic recognition of signs and a few words she hears in the village. She can understand simple spoken Kannada used for daily transactions, but she cannot read or write it. Her exposure to Hindi is mainly through religious songs and occasional media, giving her a rudimentary listening comprehension.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Rajeshwari is skilled in traditional household cooking, especially preparing Karnataka staples like jolada rotti, sambar, and sweet delicacies for festivals. She can hand‑loom basic cotton fabric and weave simple designs for household use. Her fluency in Urdu allows her to read and write letters, compose poetry, and preserve family stories in the language. She manages household finances through simple cash‑book keeping, and she is proficient in caring for young children, including basic first‑aid measures. Additionally, she tends a modest kitchen garden, knowing how to cultivate seasonal vegetables.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Cooking (Karnataka cuisine)', 'Traditional handloom weaving', 'Urdu reading & writing', 'Household cash bookkeeping', 'Basic childcare & first aid', 'Kitchen gardening']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Rajeshwari finds solace in solitary activities such as listening to Urdu ghazals and reciting poetry from classical poets like Mirza Ghalib. She enjoys tending to her kitchen garden, growing seasonal vegetables like okra, beans, and leafy greens. Cooking experiments for family celebrations give her a creative outlet. She also likes knitting simple yarn scarves during quiet evenings and watching Hindi serials on the small television set. Occasionally, she walks along the fields with her dog, using the time for personal reflection.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Urdu ghazals', 'Reciting poetry', 'Kitchen gardening', 'Experimenting with festive cooking', 'Knitting simple scarves', 'Watching Hindi TV serials', 'Walking in fields with her dog']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Rajeshwari wishes to improve her literacy in Urdu and acquire basic reading skills in Kannada so she can handle market transactions independently. She aspires to turn her cooking talent into a small tiffin‑service business that supplies packed meals to daily wage workers in nearby towns. Over the next few years she hopes to learn simple bookkeeping and, if possible, a tailoring skill that would let her sell embroidered household linens. Ultimately, she wants a stable source of income that reduces the family’s financial anxieties and gives her a sense of personal achievement.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 22-year-old female from rural Gulbarga, Karnataka, with below-primary education and Urdu as her primary language, is currently unmarried. She is a homemaker with no formal occupation, though she aspires to start a tiffin service. Her lifestyle includes occasional impulsive spending and a tendency to worry, with limited literacy in Kannada and Hindi.

  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Discharge Summary for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Urdu (code=ur, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
MATERNAL SAFETY: clinical content must be age-plausible for persona age=22. Routine ANC/delivery typically ages 18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC/PNC/contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy as a routine story.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Karnataka, district=Gulbarga.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Urdu.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 22
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Karnataka
- district: Gulbarga
- first_language: Urdu
- clinical_persona_summary: A 22-year-old female from rural Gulbarga, Karnataka, with below-primary education and Urdu as her primary language, is currently unmarried. She is a homemaker with no formal occupation, though she aspires to start a tiffin service. Her lifestyle includes occasional impulsive spending and a tendency to worry, with limited literacy in Kannada and Hindi.

Document format guide for Discharge Summary (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] DOB [[DOB|1994-06-21]] [[AGE|30]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]] | Course / advice: <concise paragraphs matching domain + persona sex/age>

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ABHA_ADDRESS — ABHA Address e.g. [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
- CASTE — Caste e.g. [[CASTE|OBC]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Gulbarga Institute of Medical Sciences]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ayesha Begum]] DOB [[DOB|2002-10-15]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-08-10]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Nair]] | Course / advice: Patient is a 22-year-old G1P0 from rural Gulbarga, Karnataka, who presented in labor on 10 August 2024. Her antenatal course was uncomplicated with regular check-ups. She was admitted with spontaneous onset of labor and delivered a healthy male infant via normal vaginal delivery at 02:30 hours on 11 August 2024. Post-delivery, the patient and neonate are stable. Uterus is well-contracted and involution is progressing normally. Lochia is serosanguinous, scant. Vital signs are stable. The patient is advised on exclusive breastfeeding, postpartum hygiene, and contraception counseling. She was counseled on family planning options and will be followed up by the ASHA worker.

For any queries, please contact the patient's mother, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Amina Begum]], at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9448012345]]. Patient's ABHA ID is [[ABHA_ADDRESS|ayesha.begum@abdm]]. Patient belongs to [[CASTE|OBC]] community and resides in [[DISTRICT|Gulbarga]].

payload.professional_persona: Rajeshwari Satari, a 22‑year‑old aspiring tiffin‑service entrepreneur, blends practical cooking expertise with hands‑on cash‑book keeping, yet their relaxed approach to planning sometimes causes missed market opportunities.
payload.linguistic_persona: Rajeshwari Satari is fluent in Urdu, reading and writing it effortlessly, but they can only grasp basic spoken Kannada and recognise a few Hindi words from songs, reflecting limited formal literacy.
payload.sports_persona: Rajeshwari Satari enjoys regular walks with her dog across the farmland and occasionally joins informal village cricket matches, cheering for Royal Challengers Bangalore, though they rarely attend a formal gym.
payload.arts_persona: Rajeshwari Satari immerses herself in Urdu ghazals by Mirza Ghalib and the melodious voice of Lata Mangeshkar, knits simple yarn scarves, and hand‑weaves modest cotton fabrics, finding artistic fulfillment both in literature and textile crafts.
payload.travel_persona: Rajeshwari Satari prefers low‑cost weekend trips by state bus to nearby towns like Gulbarga or Bidar, exploring historic forts and local markets, while dreaming of an eventual pilgrimage to Hyderabad’s old city for a ghazal evening.
payload.culinary_persona: Rajeshwari Satari delights in cooking traditional Karnataka dishes such as jolada rotti with sambar, sweet holige, and spiced okra stir‑fry, favouring fresh coriander, coconut, tamarind and jaggery, though they rely mostly on family recipes rather than formal culinary training.
payload.persona: Rajeshwari Satari is a solitary Urdu‑loving aspiring tiffin‑service provider who balances practical cooking and cash‑keeping with a habit of worrying and occasional disorganized impulse buying.
payload.cultural_background: Rajeshwari grew up in a rural village near Gulbarga, Karnataka, where the cultural landscape blends Deccani Urdu traditions with the local Kannada way of life. Her family is Hindu, but Urdu is the language spoken at home, a legacy of the erstwhile Hyderabad State that once covered this region. She celebrates Hindu festivals such as Ugadi and Diwali, while also taking part in community gatherings that feature Urdu poetry recitals. The agrarian setting, joint‑family households, and emphasis on home‑made crafts shape her everyday experience.
payload.linguistic_background: Rajeshwari’s mother tongue is Urdu, and she is fluent in speaking, reading, and writing it in the Perso‑Arabic script. Because she left school before reaching primary level, her formal literacy in Kannada and Hindi is limited to basic recognition of signs and a few words she hears in the village. She can understand simple spoken Kannada used for daily transactions, but she cannot read or write it. Her exposure to Hindi is mainly through religious songs and occasional media, giving her a rudimentary listening comprehension.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Rajeshwari is skilled in traditional household cooking, especially preparing Karnataka staples like jolada rotti, sambar, and sweet delicacies for festivals. She can hand‑loom basic cotton fabric and weave simple designs for household use. Her fluency in Urdu allows her to read and write letters, compose poetry, and preserve family stories in the language. She manages household finances through simple cash‑book keeping, and she is proficient in caring for young children, including basic first‑aid measures. Additionally, she tends a modest kitchen garden, knowing how to cultivate seasonal vegetables.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Cooking (Karnataka cuisine)', 'Traditional handloom weaving', 'Urdu reading & writing', 'Household cash bookkeeping', 'Basic childcare & first aid', 'Kitchen gardening']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Rajeshwari finds solace in solitary activities such as listening to Urdu ghazals and reciting poetry from classical poets like Mirza Ghalib. She enjoys tending to her kitchen garden, growing seasonal vegetables like okra, beans, and leafy greens. Cooking experiments for family celebrations give her a creative outlet. She also likes knitting simple yarn scarves during quiet evenings and watching Hindi serials on the small television set. Occasionally, she walks along the fields with her dog, using the time for personal reflection.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Urdu ghazals', 'Reciting poetry', 'Kitchen gardening', 'Experimenting with festive cooking', 'Knitting simple scarves', 'Watching Hindi TV serials', 'Walking in fields with her dog']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Rajeshwari wishes to improve her literacy in Urdu and acquire basic reading skills in Kannada so she can handle market transactions independently. She aspires to turn her cooking talent into a small tiffin‑service business that supplies packed meals to daily wage workers in nearby towns. Over the next few years she hopes to learn simple bookkeeping and, if possible, a tailoring skill that would let her sell embroidered household linens. Ultimately, she wants a stable source of income that reduces the family’s financial anxieties and gives her a sense of personal achievement.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 22-year-old female from rural Gulbarga, Karnataka, with below-primary education and Urdu as her primary language, is currently unmarried. She is a homemaker managing household finances and cooking, with limited formal employment. Her lifestyle includes occasional impulsive spending and a tendency to worry, while her cultural background blends Deccani Urdu traditions with local Kannada influences.

You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- state: Karnataka
- district: Gulbarga
- first_language: Urdu
- clinical_persona_summary: A 22-year-old female from rural Gulbarga, Karnataka, with below-primary education and Urdu as her primary language, is currently unmarried. She is a homemaker managing household finances and cooking, with limited formal employment. Her lifestyle includes occasional impulsive spending and a tendency to worry, while her cultural background blends Deccani Urdu traditions with local Kannada influences.

payload.generated_text:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-GUL-089]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Gulbarga]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ayesha Begum]], [[AGE|22]] / [[GENDER|Female]], District [[DISTRICT|Gulbarga]]
Reason: Persistent cough with weight loss and night sweats for 3 weeks

Dear Sir/Madam,

I am referring [[PATIENT_NAME|Ayesha Begum]], a 22-year-old female homemaker from rural Gulbarga, for further evaluation and management of suspected pulmonary tuberculosis. The patient presents with a 3-week history of productive cough, low-grade fever, night sweats, and approximately 5kg weight loss. She reports occasional chest discomfort and fatigue.

On examination, vital signs are stable. Chest auscultation reveals decreased breath sounds in the right upper zone. Given her symptoms and risk factors including close contact with a known TB case in the village, I suspect pulmonary TB.

Investigations ordered:
- Sputum for AFB smear and culture
- Chest X-ray
- Complete blood count
- Blood sugar (due to family history of diabetes)

The patient's [[MRN|MRN-2024-GUL-0456]] is attached. For any queries, please contact me at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]. Her father [[RELATIVE_NAME|Mohammed Iqbal]] can be reached at the same number.

Patient's [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P789456123]] and [[DRIVING_LICENCE|KA02 20200001234]] are available for verification.

Yours sincerely,
Dr. Rajesh Kumar
MBBS, MD (Medicine)
District Hospital Gulbarga

payload.arts_persona: Rajeshwari Satari immerses herself in Urdu ghazals by Mirza Ghalib and the melodious voice of Lata Mangeshkar, knits simple yarn scarves, and hand‑weaves modest cotton fabrics, finding artistic fulfillment both in literature and textile crafts.
payload.travel_persona: Rajeshwari Satari prefers low‑cost weekend trips by state bus to nearby towns like Gulbarga or Bidar, exploring historic forts and local markets, while dreaming of an eventual pilgrimage to Hyderabad’s old city for a ghazal evening.
payload.culinary_persona: Rajeshwari Satari delights in cooking traditional Karnataka dishes such as jolada rotti with sambar, sweet holige, and spiced okra stir‑fry, favouring fresh coriander, coconut, tamarind and jaggery, though they rely mostly on family recipes rather than formal culinary training.
payload.persona: Rajeshwari Satari is a solitary Urdu‑loving aspiring tiffin‑service provider who balances practical cooking and cash‑keeping with a habit of worrying and occasional disorganized impulse buying.
payload.cultural_background: Rajeshwari grew up in a rural village near Gulbarga, Karnataka, where the cultural landscape blends Deccani Urdu traditions with the local Kannada way of life. Her family is Hindu, but Urdu is the language spoken at home, a legacy of the erstwhile Hyderabad State that once covered this region. She celebrates Hindu festivals such as Ugadi and Diwali, while also taking part in community gatherings that feature Urdu poetry recitals. The agrarian setting, joint‑family households, and emphasis on home‑made crafts shape her everyday experience.
payload.linguistic_background: Rajeshwari’s mother tongue is Urdu, and she is fluent in speaking, reading, and writing it in the Perso‑Arabic script. Because she left school before reaching primary level, her formal literacy in Kannada and Hindi is limited to basic recognition of signs and a few words she hears in the village. She can understand simple spoken Kannada used for daily transactions, but she cannot read or write it. Her exposure to Hindi is mainly through religious songs and occasional media, giving her a rudimentary listening comprehension.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Rajeshwari is skilled in traditional household cooking, especially preparing Karnataka staples like jolada rotti, sambar, and sweet delicacies for festivals. She can hand‑loom basic cotton fabric and weave simple designs for household use. Her fluency in Urdu allows her to read and write letters, compose poetry, and preserve family stories in the language. She manages household finances through simple cash‑book keeping, and she is proficient in caring for young children, including basic first‑aid measures. Additionally, she tends a modest kitchen garden, knowing how to cultivate seasonal vegetables.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.

- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
